Unnamed: 1: Construction (Design and Management) Regulations 2015 — Client Duties
Unnamed: 4: Client must make suitable arrangements for managing the project including: allocating sufficient time and resources, ensuring duty holders are appointed, ensuring welfare facilities are in place, and ensuring H&S management arrangements exist throughout the project
Unnamed: 5: Unlimited fines, prosecution, imprisonment, stop work
Unnamed: 6: Project initiation / RIBA Stage 1
Unnamed: 7: CDM client duties checklist auto-created on project set-up, RIBA Stage 1 gate check
Unnamed: 8: Client/Programme Sponsor, PM, H&S Advisor, Delivery Partner
Unnamed: 9: Confirm governance structure → Allocate H&S budget and time → Confirm welfare arrangements → Confirm competence checks for all appointments → Sign Client CDM Duty Statement
Unnamed: 10: Signed Client CDM Duty Statement, Approved project H&S governance plan, Welfare plan confirmed, Competence check records

Unnamed: 1: CDM 2015 — Duty Holder Appointments (PD & PC)
Unnamed: 4: Principal Designer (PD) must be appointed in writing before design work begins. Principal Contractor (PC) must be appointed in writing before construction begins. Both must be competent for the scale and complexity of the project.
Unnamed: 5: Unlimited fines, prosecution, imprisonment, stop work
Unnamed: 6: Before design starts (PD) / Before construction starts (PC)
Unnamed: 7: Alert if PD/PC not assigned by target dates; appointment letter template auto-generated
Unnamed: 8: PM, Procurement, Legal, PD, PC
Unnamed: 9: Draft PD appointment letter → Confirm scope and fee → Sign and execute → Draft PC appointment letter at procurement stage → Confirm scope → Sign and execute → Store both in project document control
Unnamed: 10: Signed PD appointment letter filed in document control, Signed PC appointment letter filed in document control, Competence evidence on file for both

Unnamed: 1: CDM 2015 — Pre-Construction Information (PCI)
Unnamed: 4: Client must compile and provide Pre-Construction Information to all designers and contractors: site surveys, ground conditions, existing H&S file, utilities, asbestos data, structural information, environmental constraints
Unnamed: 5: Fines, enforcement, downstream design risk if PCI incomplete
Unnamed: 6: Before tender / Before design commences
Unnamed: 7: Reminders to upload surveys and compile PCI pack before tender issue
Unnamed: 8: Surveys team, Asset data team, Asbestos surveyor, Structural engineer, Utilities, PM
Unnamed: 9: Gather all available surveys (asbestos, utilities, structural, ground, environmental) → Compile PCI pack → Issue to all tenderers with tender documents → Version control and confirm receipt
Unnamed: 10: PCI pack compiled and issued with transmittal evidence, Version controlled in document management, Confirmation of receipt from all tenderers

Unnamed: 1: CDM 2015 — Design Risk Management
Unnamed: 4: PD must plan, manage, monitor and coordinate design work; eliminate or reduce foreseeable construction risks through design; ensure all designers comply with their CDM duties; maintain and update design risk register throughout design stages
Unnamed: 5: Fines, design rework, enforcement, liability if risk manifests on site
Unnamed: 6: Design stage — at each RIBA stage gate
Unnamed: 7: Design Risk Review task triggered at each RIBA stage freeze
Unnamed: 8: PD, Designers, PM, H&S Advisor, Contractor (two-stage where applicable)
Unnamed: 9: Run design risk workshops at each RIBA stage → Maintain design risk register → Assign risk owners and mitigation actions → Close or formally accept residual risks at stage sign-off → PD confirms DRM complete before stage gate
Unnamed: 10: Design risk register maintained and updated at each stage, Workshop minutes and attendance records, All actions closed or formally accepted with sign-off, PD stage gate sign-off evidence

Unnamed: 1: CDM 2015 — Construction Phase Plan (CPP)
Unnamed: 4: PC must prepare a Construction Phase Plan before construction starts. CPP must cover: site rules, H&S controls, RAMS framework, emergency plans, welfare logistics, and site management arrangements. CPP must be reviewed and updated as the project develops.
Unnamed: 5: Stop work; fines; enforcement notice
Unnamed: 6: Mobilisation / Before start on site
Unnamed: 7: 'No Start' gate if CPP not approved; CPP approval status field on project dashboard
Unnamed: 8: PC, Site manager, H&S Advisor, PM
Unnamed: 9: PC drafts CPP → PM and H&S Advisor review → Approve → Brief entire site team → Publish controlled copy on site → Update at each significant change
Unnamed: 10: Approved CPP filed in project document control, Briefing records and toolbox talk attendance sheets, Controlled copy on site, Review and update log

Unnamed: 1: CDM 2015 — Risk Assessments & Method Statements (RAMS)
Unnamed: 4: Suitable and sufficient RAMS must be in place for all high-risk construction activities before those activities commence. PC is responsible for reviewing, approving and ensuring workforce understands all RAMS.
Unnamed: 5: Fines; enforcement; unlimited liability if incident occurs without RAMS
Unnamed: 6: Before each high-risk or notifiable activity commences on site
Unnamed: 7: RAMS approval gate on works orders; auto-alert before planned activity start
Unnamed: 8: PC, Subcontractors, Site manager, H&S Advisor
Unnamed: 9: Collect RAMS from subcontractors → Review for suitability and sufficiency → Approve → Brief workforce → Confirm understanding (sign-off) → Keep accessible on site
Unnamed: 10: RAMS approval records, Workforce briefing sign-in sheets for each activity, RAMS accessible on site in document register

Unnamed: 1: CDM 2015 — Welfare Facilities
Unnamed: 4: Suitable welfare facilities must be in place from day one of mobilisation: toilets, washing facilities, drying room, rest area, drinking water. Standard must be maintained throughout the construction phase.
Unnamed: 5: HSE Enforcement Notice; stop work; fines
Unnamed: 6: Before any workers attend site — mobilisation day
Unnamed: 7: Welfare readiness checklist required before mobilisation sign-off
Unnamed: 8: PC, Site manager, Client
Unnamed: 9: Confirm toilet provision → Confirm washing facilities → Confirm drying facilities → Confirm rest facilities → Confirm drinking water → Record welfare inspection → Inspect periodically throughout construction
Unnamed: 10: Welfare inspection record signed by PC, Photographic evidence filed, Periodic inspection logs maintained throughout

Unnamed: 1: CDM 2015 — Site Induction & Training
Unnamed: 4: All workers must receive a site-specific induction before commencing work. Appropriate training and supervision must be in place. CSCS cards or equivalent competency evidence must be verified.
Unnamed: 5: Fines; incident liability; HSE enforcement
Unnamed: 6: First day on site for all workers; ongoing for new starters and subcontractors
Unnamed: 7: Induction due alerts for new starters; CSCS verification prompt on worker registration
Unnamed: 8: PC, Site manager, Subcontractors, Training provider
Unnamed: 9: Deliver site induction → Capture attendance on register → Verify CSCS/competency cards → Confirm supervision arrangements → Record and retain for project duration
Unnamed: 10: Induction records for all workers, Signed attendance register, Competency card evidence stored in site file

Unnamed: 1: CDM 2015 — HSE F10 Notification
Unnamed: 4: Notify HSE before construction starts if the project is notifiable: more than 30 working days with 20+ simultaneous workers OR more than 500 person-days total. Notification via F10 on HSE website. Display F10 on site.
Unnamed: 5: Fines; enforcement; criminal liability for failure to notify on notifiable project
Unnamed: 6: Before construction starts — if notifiability thresholds met
Unnamed: 7: Automatic notifiability check from project duration and headcount fields; F10 submission deadline alert
Unnamed: 8: PM, H&S Advisor, PC, Client
Unnamed: 9: Confirm project duration and max simultaneous workforce → Determine notifiability → Submit F10 on HSE website → Obtain submission reference number → Display F10 on site notice board → File in project document control
Unnamed: 10: F10 submission confirmation with HSE reference number, Displayed on site notice board (photographic evidence), Filed in project document control

Unnamed: 1: CDM 2015 — Health & Safety File
Unnamed: 4: PD must prepare and maintain the H&S File throughout the project and hand it over to the client at practical completion. The file must contain: as-built information, residual risks, O&M manuals, structural information, and any information needed for future maintenance.
Unnamed: 5: Legal exposure for client; unsafe future maintenance; enforcement
Unnamed: 6: Handover / Practical completion
Unnamed: 7: H&S File completeness checklist alert before practical completion gate
Unnamed: 8: PD, Designers, PC, FM/Asset Management team
Unnamed: 9: Compile H&S file progressively throughout project → Validate completeness at pre-handover stage → Hand over to client/FM team at practical completion → Confirm receipt in writing → Store in asset management system
Unnamed: 10: Complete indexed H&S File, Signed receipt from client/FM team, Stored in asset management system, Golden Thread inclusion (HRBs)

Unnamed: 1: Control of Asbestos Regulations 2012 (CAR 2012)
Unnamed: 4: Duty to manage asbestos in non-domestic premises and communal areas of residential buildings. Management survey required before maintenance works. R&D survey required before refurbishment/demolition. Asbestos register and management plan must be maintained. Licensed contractors required for licensable work. ASB5 notification to HSE.
Unnamed: 5: Criminal prosecution, unlimited fines, imprisonment, HSE Improvement/Prohibition Notices, unlimited civil liability
Unnamed: 6: Any works on pre-2000 buildings / Housing management in pre-2000 stock / New contractor appointment
Unnamed: 7: Pre-2000 building flag on works orders, asbestos register access alert before works, licensed contractor verification, ASB5 notification tracker
Unnamed: 8: Asset Management, Repairs team, Principal Contractor, HSE, Licensed Asbestos Contractor, Tenants
Unnamed: 9: Identify all pre-2000 buildings → Commission management surveys → Maintain asbestos registers → Circulate register to all contractors before works → Commission R&D survey before refurbishment → Appoint licensed contractor for licensable work → Submit ASB5 to HSE → Update registers periodically
Unnamed: 10: Management surveys for all pre-2000 stock, Asbestos registers on asset management system, R&D surveys before all refurbishment, Licensed contractor appointments evidenced, ASB5 notifications logged

Unnamed: 1: Legionella Risk Assessment — L8 ACOP / COSHH Regulations 2002
Unnamed: 4: Duty holders must assess and control Legionella risk in domestic water systems. Requires risk assessment of all hot and cold water systems, written control scheme, regular monitoring and flushing of low-use outlets, 5-year records. Enhanced management for communal systems.
Unnamed: 5: Criminal prosecution, unlimited fines, imprisonment; civil claims if death or illness results
Unnamed: 6: New tenancy / Post-void / Post-construction / Annual review / Risk assessment expiry
Unnamed: 7: Legionella risk assessment expiry alert, void property flushing reminder, communal system inspection scheduler
Unnamed: 8: Competent Legionella Assessor, Asset Management, Repairs team, Tenants, Housing Officers, Environmental Health
Unnamed: 9: Commission risk assessment → Develop written control scheme → Implement monitoring programme → Review every 2 years or on significant change → Enhanced monitoring for communal systems → Keep 5-year records
Unnamed: 10: Risk assessment on file, Written control scheme documented, Monthly monitoring records, Flushing records for low-use outlets, 5-year records maintained

Unnamed: 1: LOLER 1998 — Lifting Operations and Lifting Equipment Regulations
Unnamed: 4: All lifting equipment (passenger lifts, service lifts, hoists, MEWPs, cranes) must be subject to thorough examination at defined intervals by a competent person. Passenger lifts: every 6 months. Written scheme of examination required. Applies to occupied buildings and construction phase.
Unnamed: 5: HSE Improvement/Prohibition Notices, unlimited fines, prosecution, civil liability for injury
Unnamed: 6: Lift installation / 6-month examination due date / Construction phase MEWP use
Unnamed: 7: 6-monthly LOLER examination alert, written scheme review reminder, construction phase MEWP pre-use check
Unnamed: 8: Lift Maintenance Contractor, Competent Person (LOLER examiner), Asset Management, Site Manager, H&S Manager
Unnamed: 9: Identify all lifting equipment → Appoint competent person → Establish written scheme of examination → Schedule 6-monthly examinations → Implement remedial actions → Keep examination records
Unnamed: 10: Written scheme of examination in place, 6-monthly examination certificates, Examination records for all lifting equipment, Remedial actions completed and evidenced

Unnamed: 1: Building Safety Act 2022 — HRB Determination & BSR Pre-Application
Unnamed: 4: Determine at the earliest design stage whether any building in the programme meets the Higher-Risk Building (HRB) threshold: ≥18 metres or ≥7 storeys AND contains at least 2 residential units. HRBs must be registered with the BSR and are subject to the full Gateway regime. Pre-application advice from BSR is available and strongly recommended for complex projects.
Unnamed: 5: Proceeding on the basis of incorrect HRB determination can result in enforcement action, stop work, and retrospective Gateway compliance at significant cost
Unnamed: 6: RIBA Stage 0/1 — on project initiation / Site acquisition / Planning pre-application
Unnamed: 7: HRB threshold check auto-triggered on project set-up from height and storey data fields
Unnamed: 8: Development Manager, Architect, Building Control Body, BSR, Legal, Client
Unnamed: 9: Establish building height (AOD) and storey count at earliest design stage → Apply HRB threshold (≥18m or ≥7 storeys, ≥2 residential units) → If HRB: register with BSR → Contact BSR for pre-application advice → Plan for full Gateway regime in programme → Brief design team on HRB obligations → Document HRB determination in project log
Unnamed: 10: HRB determination documented and signed off by PM, BSR registration confirmation (if HRB), Pre-application advice meeting notes, Design team HRB briefing evidence

Unnamed: 1: Building Safety Act 2022 — Gateway 2 (Pre-Construction Approval)
Unnamed: 4: Gateway 2 is mandatory for all HRBs before any construction work can commence. The developer/RP must submit a full building control application to the BSR (not a private Approved Inspector), including: full plans, fire strategy, structural design, building safety case report, and construction control plan. BSR has up to 8 weeks (extendable) to assess. No construction can start without Gateway 2 approval.
Unnamed: 5: Criminal liability; stop work notices; enforcement action; unlimited fines; construction started without Gateway 2 is unlawful
Unnamed: 6: Before any construction work commences on HRB — typically RIBA Stage 4 submission
Unnamed: 7: Gateway 2 submission deadline alert (12 weeks before target start on site), BSR assessment period countdown, construction start gate blocked until Gateway 2 confirmed
Unnamed: 8: Principal Designer, Fire Engineer, Structural Engineer, BSR, Client/Developer, Legal
Unnamed: 9: Prepare full plans package → Develop fire strategy → Prepare structural design → Draft Building Safety Case Report → Prepare Construction Control Plan → Submit complete Gateway 2 application to BSR → Manage BSR queries during assessment period → Receive Gateway 2 determination → Address any conditions → Confirm approval before issuing commencement notice
Unnamed: 10: Complete Gateway 2 application submitted to BSR, BSR acknowledgement of receipt, BSR Gateway 2 approval letter (with any conditions), Conditions discharged and evidenced, Gateway 2 approval filed in Golden Thread

Unnamed: 1: Building Safety Act 2022 — Building Safety Case
Unnamed: 4: A Building Safety Case must be prepared, maintained and submitted to the BSR. It must demonstrate that the building's design adequately manages building safety risks throughout its life. The Safety Case is a live document that must be updated at each Gateway and whenever significant changes occur. A Safety Case Report summarising the case must be submitted to BSR at Gateway 2 and Gateway 3.
Unnamed: 5: Criminal liability; Gateway 2/3 refused without adequate Safety Case; enforcement action
Unnamed: 6: Preparation: RIBA Stage 3 onwards / Submission: Gateway 2 and Gateway 3 / Review: significant design change
Unnamed: 7: Safety Case update trigger on any design change affecting safety, Gateway submission deadline alert
Unnamed: 8: Fire Engineer, Structural Engineer, Principal Designer, BSR, Building Safety Manager (once appointed), Client
Unnamed: 9: Appoint competent safety case author at RIBA Stage 3 → Develop initial Building Safety Case → Update at each design stage → Prepare Safety Case Report for Gateway 2 submission → Update following any design changes during construction → Update for Gateway 3 submission → Maintain as live document post-occupation
Unnamed: 10: Building Safety Case document version controlled, Safety Case Report submitted at Gateway 2, Safety Case updated and resubmitted at Gateway 3, Design change log linked to Safety Case updates, Safety Case stored in Golden Thread

Unnamed: 1: Building Safety Act 2022 — Golden Thread of Information
Unnamed: 4: The Golden Thread is a digital, structured and version-controlled record of all building safety information for an HRB. It must be established during design, maintained throughout construction, and handed over and kept current during occupation. It includes: design drawings, specifications, safety case, O&M manuals, fire safety information, structural information, and all changes. Must be accessible to residents, BSR and the Accountable Person.
Unnamed: 5: Non-compliance with Golden Thread obligations: enforcement action, unlimited fines, Gateway refusals
Unnamed: 6: Established: RIBA Stage 2 / Maintained: throughout design and construction / Handed over: at Gateway 3
Unnamed: 7: Missing information alerts, version control audit, Golden Thread completeness checklist at each Gateway
Unnamed: 8: BIM Manager, Document Controller, Principal Designer, Principal Contractor, BSR, Accountable Person, Residents
Unnamed: 9: Establish digital Golden Thread platform at RIBA Stage 2 → Define information requirements with design team → Upload and version control all design information → Maintain during construction with as-built updates → Ensure all changes are recorded with reason and date → Prepare Golden Thread handover pack for Gateway 3 → Transfer to Accountable Person/BSM on occupation → Keep current throughout occupation
Unnamed: 10: Golden Thread platform established with access controls, Design information uploaded and version controlled, Construction as-built records maintained, Gateway 3 handover pack complete, Accountable Person confirmed as custodian, Residents given access to relevant safety information

Unnamed: 1: Building Safety Act 2022 — Accountable Person & Building Safety Manager
Unnamed: 4: For occupied HRBs, an Accountable Person (AP) must be identified and registered with the BSR. The AP is the entity responsible for safety in occupied HRBs — typically the freeholder or long leaseholder with repairing obligations. The AP must appoint a Building Safety Manager (BSM) to manage day-to-day safety. Both must be registered with BSR. AP must submit Safety Case to BSR at registration.
Unnamed: 5: Criminal liability for AP; enforcement action; unlimited fines; BSR can appoint a special measures manager if AP fails duties
Unnamed: 6: Before occupation — AP must be registered before building can be occupied / BSM appointed before Gateway 3
Unnamed: 7: AP and BSM appointment deadline alert before Gateway 3, BSR registration tracker, Safety Case submission to BSR at registration
Unnamed: 8: Accountable Person (freeholder/RP), Building Safety Manager, BSR, Solicitors, Leaseholders, Residents
Unnamed: 9: Identify the Accountable Person under BSA 2022 → Register AP with BSR → Appoint Building Safety Manager (BSM) — must be competent → Register BSM with BSR → Prepare and submit Safety Case to BSR on registration → Establish residents engagement arrangements → Set up building safety register of residents → Report to BSR annually
Unnamed: 10: AP identified and registered with BSR, BSM appointed and registered with BSR, BSM competence evidence, Safety Case submitted to BSR at registration, Annual report to BSR submitted on time, Building safety register of residents maintained

Unnamed: 1: Building Safety Act 2022 — Residents Engagement Strategy
Unnamed: 4: Registered HRBs must have a documented Residents Engagement Strategy (RES) approved by the BSR. The RES must set out: how the AP will engage with residents on building safety matters, how residents can raise safety concerns, how the AP will respond to concerns, and how residents can access safety information. The AP must act on residents' safety concerns and keep records.
Unnamed: 5: Enforcement action by BSR; failure to maintain RES is a statutory breach; residents can complain to BSR
Unnamed: 6: Before occupation / On BSR registration / Annual review
Unnamed: 7: RES review annual reminder, resident safety concern response SLA tracker, BSR compliance check flag
Unnamed: 8: Accountable Person, Building Safety Manager, Residents, BSR, Housing Management team
Unnamed: 9: Develop Residents Engagement Strategy aligned to BSR requirements → Consult residents on the strategy → Submit to BSR → Publish and communicate to all residents → Establish building safety concerns reporting channel → Respond to resident safety concerns within agreed timescales → Review and update annually → Annual report to BSR on engagement activities
Unnamed: 10: Residents Engagement Strategy document approved, BSR submission confirmation, Resident communication records, Safety concern log with response times, Annual review completed and updated, BSR annual report submitted

Unnamed: 1: Building Safety Act 2022 — Gateway 3 (Pre-Occupation Completion Certificate)
Unnamed: 4: Gateway 3 is the final sign-off before any HRB can be occupied. The developer must apply to the BSR with: as-built drawings and specifications, updated Safety Case Report, updated Golden Thread, O&M manuals, fire safety information, and evidence that the building has been built in accordance with the Gateway 2 approved plans. BSR has 8 weeks to assess. No occupation is permitted until Gateway 3 is approved.
Unnamed: 5: Criminal liability; unlimited fines; enforcement action; occupation without Gateway 3 is unlawful; building insurance may be invalidated; mortgage lenders will not lend
Unnamed: 6: At practical completion — before any occupation of any unit in the HRB
Unnamed: 7: Gateway 3 application checklist — all as-built documentation complete, Safety Case updated, Golden Thread complete; occupation gate blocked until Gateway 3 confirmed
Unnamed: 8: Principal Designer, Principal Contractor, Fire Engineer, BSR, Accountable Person, Solicitors, Homes England
Unnamed: 9: Compile as-built drawings and specifications → Update Safety Case Report → Complete Golden Thread handover pack → Prepare O&M manuals → Submit complete Gateway 3 application to BSR → Manage BSR queries → Receive Gateway 3 determination → Address any conditions → Confirm Gateway 3 approval → Notify AP and BSM → Register for occupation → Notify Homes England if grant-funded
Unnamed: 10: Complete Gateway 3 application submitted to BSR, BSR acknowledgement of receipt, BSR Gateway 3 approval (Completion Certificate), Conditions discharged and evidenced, Gateway 3 certificate filed in Golden Thread, AP and BSM notified, Homes England completion notification submitted

Unnamed: 1: Regulatory Reform (Fire Safety) Order 2005 — Initial Fire Risk Assessment
Unnamed: 2: Fire and Rescue Authority / Responsible Person
Unnamed: 4: The Responsible Person must ensure a suitable and sufficient Fire Risk Assessment (FRA) is conducted for all non-domestic premises and common areas of residential buildings before occupation. The FRA must identify all fire hazards, evaluate risks to relevant persons, record findings, implement control measures, and plan for emergency action.
Unnamed: 5: Fines, prosecution, building closure, enforcement notice, unlimited fines, imprisonment
Unnamed: 6: Before occupation of building / Any change to building use, structure, or occupancy / Significant works completed
Unnamed: 7: FRA completion reminder before occupation gate, significant works trigger for FRA review, Responsible Person appointment confirmation
Unnamed: 8: Responsible Person, Fire Risk Assessor (competent/third party), Fire and Rescue Authority, Building Management team, Residents
Unnamed: 9: Appoint competent Responsible Person → Appoint qualified Fire Risk Assessor → Conduct FRA covering all fire hazards, means of escape, fire detection, fire suppression, firefighting equipment, and emergency planning → Record findings in writing → Implement all action plan items → File FRA in building records → Brief all relevant persons
Unnamed: 10: Signed FRA report on file with date, All FRA action plan items implemented and evidenced, Responsible Person confirmed in writing, FRA filed in building records, Staff briefing evidence

Unnamed: 1: Regulatory Reform (Fire Safety) Order 2005 — Fire Strategy (New Build / Major Refurbishment)
Unnamed: 2: Building Control / Fire and Rescue Authority
Unnamed: 4: For new build and major refurbishment schemes, a fire strategy document must be prepared by a Fire Engineer at RIBA Stage 2 and developed throughout the design. The fire strategy must be approved by the building control body and submitted as part of the building control application. For HRBs, the fire strategy must also support the Building Safety Case.
Unnamed: 5: Building control stop work; completion certificate refused; Fire and Rescue Authority objection; occupation prevented; criminal and civil liability
Unnamed: 6: RIBA Stage 2 fire strategy commencement / Building control application submission / Structural frame stage / Completion stage
Unnamed: 7: Fire strategy sign-off gate at RIBA Stage 3, Fire and Rescue Authority consultation tracker for residential buildings >18m
Unnamed: 8: Fire Engineer, Architect, Principal Designer, Building Control, Fire and Rescue Authority, BSR (HRBs)
Unnamed: 9: Appoint Fire Engineer at RIBA Stage 2 → Develop fire strategy → Submit to building control for plan check approval → Consult Fire and Rescue Authority as statutory consultee (residential >18m) → Design incorporates all fire strategy elements: compartmentation, protected routes, detection, suppression → Update fire strategy at each RIBA stage → Final sign-off at completion → Include in H&S File and Golden Thread
Unnamed: 10: Fire Engineer appointment confirmed, Fire strategy approved by building control at plan check, Fire and Rescue Authority consultation response filed, Fire stopping and compartmentation inspection records, Final fire strategy sign-off at completion, Included in H&S File and Golden Thread (HRBs)

Unnamed: 1: Regulatory Reform (Fire Safety) Order 2005 — Interim Fire Safety During Construction
Unnamed: 2: Responsible Person / HSE / Fire and Rescue Authority
Unnamed: 4: Where construction works take place in occupied buildings, an Interim Fire Risk Management Plan (IFRMP) must be prepared and implemented before works commence. The IFRMP must address: temporary fire detection, means of escape during works, hot works permit system, storage of flammable materials, fire suppression during construction, and liaison with Fire and Rescue Authority.
Unnamed: 5: Enforcement notice, stop work, fines, prosecution, unlimited liability if fire causes death or injury in occupied building
Unnamed: 6: Before commencement of any works in occupied residential building
Unnamed: 7: Occupied works flag triggers IFRMP requirement before site start clearance
Unnamed: 8: Responsible Person, Principal Contractor, Fire Risk Assessor, Fire and Rescue Authority, Residents, Housing Management
Unnamed: 9: Confirm building is occupied during works → Appoint competent Interim FRA author → Develop IFRMP → Brief all residents and building occupants → Implement temporary fire detection and suppression measures → Establish hot works permit system → Notify Fire and Rescue Authority of works → Review and update IFRMP at each construction phase change → Maintain fire log throughout
Unnamed: 10: IFRMP prepared and signed off before works commence, Resident briefing records, Temporary fire safety measures installation evidence, Hot works permit system records, Fire and Rescue Authority notification, Periodic IFRMP review records

Unnamed: 1: Fire Safety (England) Regulations 2022 — Ongoing Inspection Regime
Unnamed: 2: Responsible Person / Fire and Rescue Authority
Unnamed: 4: All multi-occupied residential buildings from January 2023: share fire safety information with residents. Buildings >11m: quarterly checks of fire doors in common areas and annual checks of all flat entrance doors. Buildings ≥18m: monthly checks of evacuation lifts, fire doors, and fire safety equipment; annual checks of all flat entrance doors. All inspection records must be kept.
Unnamed: 5: Criminal prosecution under FSO 2005, unlimited fine, imprisonment
Unnamed: 6: January 2023 commencement date (in force) / Building height threshold determines inspection frequency
Unnamed: 7: Quarterly inspection scheduler for >11m blocks, monthly inspection scheduler for ≥18m blocks, resident information pack issuance tracker, annual flat entrance door inspection reminder
Unnamed: 8: Responsible Person, Fire Safety Manager, Repairs team, Residents, Fire Door Inspector
Unnamed: 9: Classify all multi-residential blocks by height → Issue resident fire safety information packs → Programme quarterly fire door checks (>11m) → Programme monthly equipment checks (≥18m) → Programme annual flat entrance door inspections (≥18m) → Log all inspections with date, operative and outcome → Implement remedial actions within agreed timescales → Report to Board
Unnamed: 10: Resident information packs issued and logged, Quarterly inspection records for all >11m blocks, Monthly check records for all ≥18m blocks, Annual flat door inspection records, Remedial action log with completion evidence, Board compliance reports

Unnamed: 1: Regulatory Reform (Fire Safety) Order 2005 — FRA Periodic Review & Reassessment
Unnamed: 2: Responsible Person / Fire and Rescue Authority
Unnamed: 4: The FRA must be reviewed regularly and whenever there is reason to suspect it is no longer valid — including: significant changes to premises, change in occupancy, significant works, fire incident, near-miss, or change of Responsible Person. Annual review recommended for residential buildings. A full reassessment by a competent assessor every 3-5 years or following significant change.
Unnamed: 5: Enforcement action for failure to maintain current FRA; fines; prosecution; liability if outdated FRA contributed to fire incident
Unnamed: 6: Annual FRA review / Post-significant works / Post-fire incident or near miss / Change of occupancy / Change of Responsible Person
Unnamed: 7: Annual FRA review reminder, post-works FRA review trigger, fire incident automatic FRA reassessment flag
Unnamed: 8: Responsible Person, Fire Risk Assessor, Building Management, Residents, Fire and Rescue Authority
Unnamed: 9: Set annual FRA review date → Conduct desktop review annually → Trigger full reassessment after any significant change → Appoint competent assessor for full reassessment → Update FRA with new findings → Implement revised action plan → Confirm Responsible Person is current → File updated FRA in building records → Notify residents of any significant changes affecting their safety
Unnamed: 10: Annual FRA review log, Full reassessment reports at appropriate intervals, Updated FRA with action plan, Revised action plan implementation evidence, Current Responsible Person confirmed, Updated FRA in building records

Unnamed: 1: Building Regulations 2010 — Building Control Application
Unnamed: 4: Before any building work commences, submit either a Full Plans application (plans approved before works — required for complex/HRB work) or a Building Notice (work can start within 48 hours). From October 2023, HRBs must use the Building Safety Regulator for building control.
Unnamed: 5: Stop work; enforcement notice; work opened up; unlimited fines; completion certificate refused
Unnamed: 6: RIBA Stage 4 completion / At least 48 hours before commencement for Building Notice
Unnamed: 7: Building control application submission alert at RIBA Stage 4, HRB routing check (BSR vs private AI)
Unnamed: 8: Architect, Principal Designer, Contractor, LABC or AI / BSR (HRBs), Client
Unnamed: 9: Determine Full Plans or Building Notice route → Confirm HRB status (BSR required if HRB) → Prepare and submit application with drawings and calculations → Pay fee → Await plan check approval → Address plan check comments → Confirm approval before commencement
Unnamed: 10: Application submitted with proof of receipt, Fee payment confirmed, Building control body confirmed with contact details, Plan check approval received, Plan check comments addressed

Unnamed: 1: Building Regulations 2010 — Commencement Notice
Unnamed: 4: The building control body must be notified on or before the day work starts on site. Without notification there is no valid inspection record. On HRB projects, Gateway 2 must be in hand before a valid commencement notice is issued.
Unnamed: 5: Completion certificate refused; enforcement notice; work opened up at contractor's cost; programme delay
Unnamed: 6: Day of or immediately before commencement on site
Unnamed: 7: Commencement notice reminder linked to programme start date, Gateway 2 clearance check for HRBs before notice fires
Unnamed: 8: PM, Principal Contractor, Building Control Body, Client
Unnamed: 9: Confirm notification method with building control body → For HRBs: confirm Gateway 2 approval in hand → Notify on or before commencement day → Obtain and file confirmation of receipt → Log commencement date in project document control
Unnamed: 10: Commencement notice submitted on or before start, Receipt from building control body, Gateway 2 confirmed (HRB), Notice filed in document control with date stamp

Unnamed: 1: Building Regulations 2010 — Staged Site Inspections
Unnamed: 4: Inspections at defined statutory stages: foundations before concrete pour, DPC, floor slab, drainage before backfilling, structural frame/roof, pre-occupation. Work must not be covered until inspection is done or formally waived. Minimum 24-hour notice to inspector.
Unnamed: 5: Stop work; enforcement to open up covered work at contractor's cost; completion certificate refused; unlimited fines
Unnamed: 6: Each statutory notification stage reached on site
Unnamed: 7: Inspection booking reminders linked to programme milestones, failed inspection remedial action tracker
Unnamed: 8: Principal Contractor, Site Manager, Building Control Inspector, Structural Engineer, Architect
Unnamed: 9: Agree inspection schedule with building control body → Programme notifications into construction programme → Give 24-hour minimum notice before each stage → Attend inspection → Record outcome and remedial actions → Complete remedials before progressing → Maintain complete inspection log
Unnamed: 10: Agreed inspection schedule, All stages inspected with signed records, No stages covered without inspection/waiver, Inspection outcome records, Complete inspection log

Unnamed: 1: Building Regulations 2010 Part B — Fire Safety
Unnamed: 2: LABC / Approved Inspector / Fire and Rescue Authority
Unnamed: 4: Part B requires: adequate means of escape, limitation of internal and external fire spread, and firefighting facilities. For residential buildings: protected escape routes, compartmentation, fire detection, sprinklers where applicable. Part B compliance demonstrated to building control throughout the project.
Unnamed: 5: Building control stop work; completion certificate refused; occupation prevented; criminal and civil liability if fire in non-compliant building
Unnamed: 6: RIBA Stage 2/3 fire strategy / Plan check / Frame stage / Completion
Unnamed: 7: Fire strategy sign-off gate at RIBA Stage 3, compartmentation inspection scheduling trigger
Unnamed: 8: Fire Engineer, Architect, Principal Designer, Principal Contractor, Building Control, Fire and Rescue Authority, BSR (HRBs)
Unnamed: 9: Appoint Fire Engineer at RIBA Stage 2 → Develop fire strategy → Submit to building control → Consult FRA as statutory consultee (residential >18m) → Compartmentation inspections at frame stage → Fire stopping inspections throughout → Final fire compliance inspection → Obtain Part B sign-off
Unnamed: 10: Fire strategy approved by building control, FRA consultation response, Compartmentation inspection records, Fire stopping records, Sprinkler commissioning certificate (where applicable), Final Part B sign-off, Included in H&S File and Golden Thread

Unnamed: 1: Building Regulations 2010 Part L — Energy Performance
Unnamed: 4: Part L requires: compliance with National Calculation Methodology (SAP 10.2), achieving Target Emission Rate (TER) and Target Fabric Energy Efficiency Rate (TFEER). As-built SAP calculations required at completion. All new homes must achieve minimum EPC Band B.
Unnamed: 5: Completion certificate refused; enforcement; rework at significant cost; delay to occupation; grant condition breach
Unnamed: 6: RIBA Stage 3: design SAP → Practical completion: as-built SAP → Before first occupation: EPC lodged
Unnamed: 7: Design-stage SAP gate at RIBA Stage 3, as-built SAP deadline alert at practical completion, EPC lodgement confirmation before occupation
Unnamed: 8: SAP/Part L Assessor, Architect, M&E Engineer, Principal Contractor, Building Control, EPC Assessor
Unnamed: 9: Appoint SAP assessor at RIBA Stage 2 → Produce design SAP → Submit to building control → Confirm TER/TFEER compliance → At completion: produce as-built SAP → Submit to building control → Lodge EPC on national register → Provide EPC to occupants
Unnamed: 10: Design SAP submitted and approved, TER/TFEER compliance confirmed, As-built SAP submitted at practical completion, EPC Band B minimum achieved, EPC lodged on national register before occupation, SAP filed in H&S File

Unnamed: 1: Building Regulations 2010 — Completion Certificate & Final Certification
Unnamed: 4: On completion, the building control body carries out a final inspection and issues: Completion Certificate (LABC), Final Certificate (AI), or Gateway 3 (BSR for HRBs). This is the legal confirmation of Building Regulations compliance. No unit can be lawfully occupied without this in hand.
Unnamed: 5: Occupation without certificate: enforcement, unlimited fines, criminal liability; units unsellable or unmortgageable; insurance invalidated; grant conditions breached
Unnamed: 6: All staged inspections complete / Outstanding items resolved / Before first occupation
Unnamed: 7: Completion certificate application checklist gate, occupation restriction flag until certificate received, Homes England notification trigger
Unnamed: 8: Principal Contractor, Building Control Body, Architect, Client, RP, Homes England, Solicitors
Unnamed: 9: Confirm all staged inspections complete with no outstanding failures → Resolve all outstanding items → Submit as-built drawings, as-built SAP, fire strategy and outstanding docs → Apply for final inspection → Attend final inspection → Address final items → Receive Completion / Final / Gateway 3 Certificate → Notify Homes England → File in document control → Include in H&S File → No occupation until certificate in hand
Unnamed: 10: All staged inspection records complete, As-built drawings, SAP and fire strategy submitted, Completion Certificate / Gateway 3 received and filed, First occupation not permitted until certificate in hand, Homes England practical completion notification submitted

Unnamed: 1: Town & Country Planning Act 1990 — Pre-Application Engagement
Unnamed: 4: Pre-application engagement with the LPA before submitting a planning application reduces risk of refusal and conditions. For major development, EIA screening direction should be requested. Listed buildings and conservation areas require early Historic England engagement. Pre-application advice is a material consideration.
Unnamed: 5: Abortive application costs; refusal risk without pre-application engagement; relationship risk with LPA
Unnamed: 6: RIBA Stage 1/2 — before planning application preparation
Unnamed: 7: Pre-application engagement reminder at RIBA Stage 2 start, EIA screening threshold check
Unnamed: 8: Planning Consultant, Architect, LPA Planning Officer, Historic England (listed/conservation), Highway Authority, Environment Agency
Unnamed: 9: Confirm pre-application engagement requirement with planning consultant → Request EIA screening direction if likely Schedule 2 development → Submit pre-application enquiry to LPA → Attend pre-application meeting → Record LPA officer advice → Apply for EIA scoping opinion if required → Incorporate LPA guidance into scheme design → Document pre-application correspondence
Unnamed: 10: LPA pre-application response on file, EIA screening direction received (if applicable), Scoping opinion received (if applicable), Pre-application meeting notes filed, LPA advice incorporated in scheme design

Unnamed: 1: Town & Country Planning Act 1990 — Planning Application Submission
Unnamed: 4: A planning application must be submitted and approved before development commences. The application must include: application forms, site location plan, block plan, design and access statement, and all required supporting documents. Statutory consultees must be consulted. Application must be validated before the statutory determination period begins (13 weeks for major applications).
Unnamed: 5: Enforcement notice; stop development; demolition order; financial penalties; reputational damage with LPA
Unnamed: 6: Pre-construction — required before any material operations on site
Unnamed: 7: Planning application submission status tracker, statutory determination period countdown, consultee response alert
Unnamed: 8: Planning Consultant, Architect, Transport Consultant, Landscape Architect, Heritage Consultant, LPA, Statutory Consultees
Unnamed: 9: Prepare full application documents → Submit to LPA → Await validation → Confirm validated date → Monitor statutory consultation period → Respond to consultee objections → Attend planning committee if required → Receive planning decision → Appeal if refused → File decision notice in project document control
Unnamed: 10: Planning application submitted and validated, Decision notice received (approval), Appeal submitted and determined (if applicable), Decision notice filed in project document control, All planning conditions listed and logged

Unnamed: 1: Town & Country Planning Act 1990 — Planning Conditions Monitoring & Discharge
Unnamed: 4: All planning conditions must be identified, catalogued and discharged in accordance with their requirements. Pre-commencement conditions must be discharged before any work starts. Conditions may cover: pre-commencement surveys, materials approval, construction management plan, landscaping, drainage, archaeology, ecology, highways, hours of working. Non-compliance is a breach of planning permission.
Unnamed: 5: Planning enforcement notice; stop work; breach of planning condition; fines; injunction; reputational damage with LPA
Unnamed: 6: Pre-commencement: before any works start / By the dates or stages specified in each condition
Unnamed: 7: Pre-commencement condition discharge tracker with gate before site start, condition discharge application deadline alert by condition type
Unnamed: 8: Planning Consultant, Architect, LPA, All specialist consultants (ecology, archaeology, highways, drainage, materials)
Unnamed: 9: Extract all conditions from planning decision notice → Categorise: pre-commencement, pre-occupation, ongoing → Log all conditions in compliance tracker with discharge deadline → Discharge all pre-commencement conditions before site start → Submit discharge of condition applications to LPA with supporting evidence → Await LPA approval → Discharge ongoing conditions at relevant milestones → File all discharge confirmations
Unnamed: 10: All conditions extracted and logged in compliance tracker, Pre-commencement conditions all discharged with LPA written confirmation, Condition discharge applications submitted for all conditions, LPA written confirmation for every discharge, No conditions outstanding at occupation

Unnamed: 1: Planning (Listed Buildings and Conservation Areas) Act 1990 — Heritage Consents
Unnamed: 4: Listed Building Consent (LBC) required for any works affecting the character of a listed building — both internal and external. Conservation area controls restrict demolition and some permitted development. Grade I and II* applications require Historic England as statutory consultee. Criminal offence to carry out works without LBC.
Unnamed: 5: Criminal offence: unlimited fine and/or 2 years imprisonment; enforcement notice requiring reversal; works cannot be retrospectively authorised
Unnamed: 6: Any building on heritage register / Any site in conservation area / Pre-application design stage
Unnamed: 7: Heritage asset check on project initiation, LBC application progress tracker, Historic England consultation deadline alert
Unnamed: 8: Planning Consultant, Heritage/Conservation Architect, Historic England, LPA, Design team, Principal Contractor
Unnamed: 9: Check statutory heritage register and conservation area status → Commission heritage impact assessment → Pre-application consultation with Historic England (Grade I/II*) → Submit LBC application alongside planning permission → Await LBC determination → Discharge LBC conditions before works commence → Brief contractor on heritage constraints → Post-works inspection
Unnamed: 10: Heritage register and conservation area search results on file, Heritage impact assessment completed, Pre-application consultation evidence (Grade I/II*), LBC granted, LBC conditions discharged, Contractor heritage briefing records

Unnamed: 1: Planning Act 2008 / CIL Regulations 2010 — Community Infrastructure Levy
Unnamed: 4: CIL charged by LPAs on new floorspace. Social housing exemption (charitable relief) must be claimed BEFORE commencement notice — if not claimed before commencement the exemption is permanently and irrecoverably lost. CIL must be paid within 60 days of commencement. S106 contributions are separate.
Unnamed: 5: Loss of exemption (permanent); surcharge of 20% of CIL liability; late payment surcharge; enforcement action preventing occupation
Unnamed: 6: Any commencement on site requiring planning permission where LPA has a CIL charging schedule
Unnamed: 7: CIL exemption claim pre-commencement mandatory check gate, commencement notice trigger, 60-day CIL payment countdown
Unnamed: 8: Development Manager, Planning Consultant, Legal, Finance, LPA
Unnamed: 9: Check if LPA has CIL charging schedule → Determine CIL liability → Assess social housing / charitable relief exemption eligibility → Submit exemption claim to LPA BEFORE commencement → Receive LPA exemption determination → Serve commencement notice on day of commencement → Pay any applicable CIL within 60 days → File all records
Unnamed: 10: CIL liability assessment documented, Exemption claim submitted and acknowledged by LPA before commencement, LPA exemption determination letter on file, Commencement notice served, CIL payment within 60 days (if applicable)

Unnamed: 1: Environment Act 2021 — Biodiversity Net Gain (BNG)
Unnamed: 4: Mandatory 10% BNG applies to all planning permissions in England from February 2024 (major sites) and April 2024 (small sites). Biodiversity Metric 4.0 assessment required at planning stage. Biodiversity Gain Plan must be submitted with planning application. On-site delivery or statutory credit purchase required. 30-year management and monitoring obligation.
Unnamed: 5: Planning permission cannot be granted without BNG compliance; enforcement; financial penalties; 30-year management plan breach
Unnamed: 6: Planning application submission for any development from February/April 2024 / Pre-application ecology stage
Unnamed: 7: BNG Metric 4.0 assessment trigger at planning stage, credit purchase alert if on-site delivery insufficient, 30-year management plan monitoring reminder
Unnamed: 8: Ecologist (BNG specialist), Planning Consultant, LPA, Natural England, Design team
Unnamed: 9: Appoint qualified ecologist at pre-application stage → Conduct pre-development biodiversity baseline assessment → Complete Biodiversity Metric 4.0 calculation → Identify on-site habitat enhancement → If insufficient, purchase statutory credits → Prepare Biodiversity Gain Plan → Submit with planning application → Secure 30-year management agreement → Annual monitoring reports
Unnamed: 10: Pre-development habitat baseline survey, Biodiversity Metric 4.0 calculation, Biodiversity Gain Plan approved by LPA, Credit purchase receipts (if applicable), 30-year management agreement in place, Annual monitoring reports submitted

Unnamed: 1: Procurement Act 2023 — Procurement Strategy & Route Determination
Unnamed: 4: Before commencing any procurement, the contracting authority must determine: the procurement route (open, restricted, competitive flexible), whether the contract is above the Procurement Act 2023 works threshold (£5.372m), whether a framework can be used, and whether the procurement is covered by the Act. Procurement strategy must be documented and approved before market engagement.
Unnamed: 5: Legal challenge to procurement strategy; contract void; debarment; failure to publish required notices
Unnamed: 6: Before any market engagement or tender preparation
Unnamed: 7: Procurement route determination checklist on project set-up, above-threshold flag triggers Procurement Act 2023 compliance workflow
Unnamed: 8: Procurement Lead, Legal, Finance, Programme Manager, Crown Commercial Service, Cabinet Office
Unnamed: 9: Determine estimated contract value → Apply Procurement Act 2023 threshold test (works: £5.372m) → Select procurement route → Confirm framework availability and suitability → Document procurement strategy → Obtain internal approval → Plan for FIND/transparency notices → Confirm social value requirements and weighting
Unnamed: 10: Procurement strategy document approved, Threshold calculation evidenced, Procurement route confirmed, Framework suitability assessment (if applicable), Internal approval sign-off, Social value requirements documented

Unnamed: 1: Procurement Act 2023 — Market Engagement & Specification
Unnamed: 4: For above-threshold procurements, a Prior Information Notice (PIN) / Preliminary Market Engagement Notice must be published on the Find a Tender Service (FTS). Market engagement must be conducted transparently and findings documented. Tender specification, evaluation criteria and methodology must be prepared before tender issue. Selection questionnaire requirements must comply with the Act.
Unnamed: 5: Procedural challenge at tender stage; judicial review; loss of procurement competition; reputational damage
Unnamed: 6: After procurement strategy approved / Before tender issue
Unnamed: 7: PIN publication reminder, market engagement findings documentation reminder, specification quality review gate before tender issue
Unnamed: 8: Procurement Lead, Technical Advisor, Legal, Market participants, Crown Commercial Service
Unnamed: 9: Publish PIN/market engagement notice on FTS (if appropriate) → Conduct market engagement: industry events, 1-to-1 briefings → Document all engagement and treat all participants equally → Prepare specification, scope of works and contract terms → Prepare evaluation criteria and weighting → Prepare selection questionnaire → Obtain internal approval for tender documents → Issue to market
Unnamed: 10: PIN published on FTS (if applicable), Market engagement findings documented with equal treatment evidence, Approved specification and scope of works, Evaluation criteria and weightings approved, Selection questionnaire prepared, Internal approval for tender issue

Unnamed: 1: Procurement Act 2023 — Tender Process & Evaluation
Unnamed: 4: The tender process must comply with the Procurement Act 2023 (from February 2025). For the competitive flexible procedure: issue of Invitation to Tender (ITT), standstill period obligations, evaluation methodology must be applied correctly and consistently, moderator independence, evaluation records retained. Award decision must be defensible.
Unnamed: 5: Legal challenge from unsuccessful tenderers; procurement suspended; contract void; reputational damage; legal costs
Unnamed: 6: Tender issue to evaluation and moderation
Unnamed: 7: Tender return deadline alert, evaluation period tracker, standstill period countdown, evaluation moderation completion reminder
Unnamed: 8: Procurement Lead, Evaluation Panel (minimum 3 members), Moderator, Legal, Technical Advisor
Unnamed: 9: Issue ITT with evaluation criteria, weighting and methodology → Receive and open tenders → Conduct evaluation by criteria → Moderate evaluation → Document all evaluation records → Identify preferred bidder → Apply exclusion grounds check → Check Debarment Register → Prepare award recommendation → Obtain internal approval → Notify award intention
Unnamed: 10: ITT issued with full evaluation methodology, Tender return records, Evaluation scoring sheets for all evaluators, Moderation records, Debarment Register check evidence, Award recommendation approved

Unnamed: 1: Procurement Act 2023 — Contract Award & Transparency Notices
Unnamed: 4: On award, a Contract Award Notice (Transparency Notice) must be published on the Find a Tender Service within 30 days of award. For contracts >£5m, KPIs must be set at the outset, published, and performance reported annually. Standstill period (minimum 8 working days) must be observed after notifying unsuccessful bidders before contract execution.
Unnamed: 5: Legal challenge during standstill period; contract void if standstill not observed; failure to publish transparency notices: enforcement and reputational damage
Unnamed: 6: On notification of award intention / On contract execution
Unnamed: 7: Standstill period countdown (8 working days), transparency notice publication deadline alert (30 days from award), KPI publication reminder on contract execution
Unnamed: 8: Procurement Lead, Legal, Finance, All tenderers (unsuccessful notification), Crown Commercial Service, Cabinet Office
Unnamed: 9: Notify all tenderers of award intention simultaneously → Observe standstill period (minimum 8 working days) → Address any standstill challenges → Execute contract → Publish Contract Award Notice (Transparency Notice) on FTS within 30 days → Set and publish KPIs for contracts >£5m → Brief contract management team → File all procurement records for minimum 7 years
Unnamed: 10: Simultaneous notification to all tenderers evidenced, Standstill period observed (8 working days), Contract executed, Transparency Notice published on FTS within 30 days, KPIs set and published (contracts >£5m), Procurement records filed

Unnamed: 1: Procurement Act 2023 / Contract — Contract Management & KPI Reporting
Unnamed: 4: Throughout the contract: KPI performance must be monitored, reported and published annually (contracts >£5m). Change management must be within permitted modifications. Contract extensions must be compliant with the Act. Payment terms must be met (30-day payment standard). Subcontractor payment chains must be monitored.
Unnamed: 5: KPI non-publication: enforcement; non-compliant modifications: contract void; payment failures: reputational damage; loss of future contracts
Unnamed: 6: Ongoing throughout contract period / Annual KPI reporting cycle
Unnamed: 7: Annual KPI performance report publication reminder, payment term SLA tracker, contract modification compliance checker
Unnamed: 8: Contract Manager, Finance, Prime Contractor, Subcontractors, Crown Commercial Service
Unnamed: 9: Monitor KPI performance quarterly → Prepare annual KPI performance report → Publish on FTS annually (contracts >£5m) → Manage all contract changes within permitted modification limits → Monitor 30-day payment standard compliance → Report subcontractor payment performance → Manage contract to programme and quality milestones → Close out contract and publish completion notice
Unnamed: 10: Quarterly KPI monitoring records, Annual KPI performance report published on FTS, Contract modification register (all changes within permitted limits), Payment SLA compliance records, Contract close-out notice published

Unnamed: 1: Modern Slavery Act 2015 — Supply Chain Compliance
Unnamed: 4: Organisations with annual turnover of £36m+ must publish an annual Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking Statement: Board-approved, Director-signed, published on website within 6 months of financial year end. Supply chain due diligence required — particularly for construction, labour, and international supply chains.
Unnamed: 5: Court injunction for non-publication; significant reputational damage; loss of public contracts; Companies House enforcement
Unnamed: 6: Annual statement due date / New major contractor appointment / Procurement exercise
Unnamed: 7: Annual statement publication deadline reminder, supply chain questionnaire trigger on contractor appointment, Board approval workflow
Unnamed: 8: CEO/Board Director, Procurement team, Legal, HR, Supply chain, Company Secretary
Unnamed: 9: Review and update Modern Slavery risk assessment annually → Issue modern slavery questionnaires to all significant contractors → Review responses and escalate concerns → Draft annual Modern Slavery Statement → Board approval and Director sign-off → Publish on company website within 6 months of year end → Submit to Companies House register → Staff training on identifying modern slavery
Unnamed: 10: Annual statement published on website with Director signature, Board approval minutes, MSA questionnaire responses from all significant contractors, Risk assessment documented, Companies House submission confirmed, Staff training records

Unnamed: 1: T&CP (EIA) Regulations 2017 — Environmental Impact Assessment
Unnamed: 4: EIA required for Schedule 1 development (mandatory) and Schedule 2 development exceeding significance thresholds screened by LPA. Residential development of 150+ units, sites >5 hectares, or in sensitive areas typically requires EIA. Screening direction should be requested pre-application. Environmental Statement must cover all significant effects.
Unnamed: 5: Planning permission invalid without required EIA; judicial review; planning enforcement; reputational damage
Unnamed: 6: Planning application for Schedule 2 development / Site >0.5 hectares / Sensitive location / Pre-application
Unnamed: 7: EIA screening threshold check on planning application submission, scoping opinion deadline alert, Environmental Statement chapter completion tracker
Unnamed: 8: Environmental Consultant, Planning Consultant, LPA, Environment Agency, Natural England, Historic England
Unnamed: 9: Determine if Schedule 1 or 2 development → Request EIA screening direction from LPA → If screened in, request scoping opinion → Commission Environmental Statement: ecology, noise, transport, flood, heritage, air quality → Statutory consultation → Submit ES with planning application → Respond to consultee queries → Discharge EIA conditions post-permission
Unnamed: 10: Screening direction from LPA on file, Scoping opinion received, Environmental Statement submitted with planning application, Statutory consultee responses addressed, EIA conditions discharged with LPA confirmation

Unnamed: 1: Flood Risk Regulations 2009 / NPPF — Flood Risk Assessment & SuDS
Unnamed: 2: Environment Agency / Lead Local Flood Authority
Unnamed: 4: Sites in Flood Zone 2 or 3 require a Flood Risk Assessment (FRA) as mandatory part of planning application. Sequential Test required. Exception Test required for Flood Zone 3b. SuDS approval required from LLFA for new drainage systems in most developments.
Unnamed: 5: Planning refused without required FRA; enforcement; development proceeds at own risk; insurance implications
Unnamed: 6: Site in or adjacent to Flood Zone 2 or 3 / New drainage system proposed / Pre-application
Unnamed: 7: Flood Zone check on site acquisition, FRA requirement flag on planning application, SuDS approval deadline alert
Unnamed: 8: Flood Risk / Drainage Engineer, Environment Agency, Lead Local Flood Authority, Planning Consultant, LPA
Unnamed: 9: Check Environment Agency Flood Map for all sites → Commission FRA for Flood Zone 2/3 sites → Complete Sequential Test → Complete Exception Test if Flood Zone 3b → Design SuDS drainage scheme → Submit SuDS approval to LLFA → Submit FRA with planning application → Discharge flood risk planning conditions
Unnamed: 10: Flood Zone classification for all sites, FRA prepared and submitted, Sequential and Exception Test evidence, SuDS approval from LLFA, Flood risk conditions discharged with LPA confirmation

Unnamed: 1: Wildlife & Countryside Act 1981 / Habitats Regulations 2017 — Protected Species & Ecology
Unnamed: 4: Criminal offence to harm protected species or their habitats without licence. Phase 1 Habitat Survey required on any site with habitat potential. Phase 2 survey required if species confirmed or likely. European Protected Species (EPS) licence from Natural England required if works harm EPS.
Unnamed: 5: Criminal prosecution, unlimited fines, imprisonment up to 5 years, works stoppage, planning enforcement
Unnamed: 6: Any site with buildings (bats), trees, water, rough grassland or scrub / Pre-application / Pre-construction clearance
Unnamed: 7: Ecology survey season calendar alert, EPS licence application deadline tracker, pre-clearance ecological check reminder
Unnamed: 8: Ecologist (licensed if EPS involved), Natural England, Planning Consultant, LPA, Principal Contractor
Unnamed: 9: Commission Phase 1 Habitat Survey → Commission Phase 2 surveys if required (bat, GCN, badger, nesting birds) → Apply for EPS licence from Natural England if species confirmed → Implement method statements and mitigation → Supervise ecological clearance works → Post-works monitoring as required
Unnamed: 10: Phase 1 survey report, Phase 2 survey reports (where required), Natural England EPS licence (where required), Ecological method statement, Pre-clearance sign-off by ecologist, Post-works monitoring reports

Unnamed: 1: Environmental Protection Act 1990 — Construction Waste Management
Unnamed: 4: All construction waste must be managed lawfully: waste must only be transported by registered waste carriers, waste transfer notes must accompany all waste movements and be retained for 2 years, hazardous waste must be separated and consigned correctly, and a site waste management plan (SWMP) should be maintained. No waste to be deposited on unlicensed land.
Unnamed: 5: Fines, prosecution, enforcement notice, costs of unlawful deposit remediation
Unnamed: 6: Construction phase — from mobilisation
Unnamed: 7: Waste carrier registration check on contractor appointment, waste transfer note upload reminder monthly, hazardous waste consignment note tracker
Unnamed: 8: Principal Contractor, Site Manager, Waste Carriers, Environmental Officer, Environment Agency
Unnamed: 9: Confirm all waste contractors are registered waste carriers → Implement site waste management plan → Segregate waste streams on site → Ensure waste transfer notes accompany all waste movements → Retain waste transfer notes for 2 years → Correct consignment of hazardous waste → Conduct periodic waste audit → Report waste volumes quarterly
Unnamed: 10: Registered waste carrier evidence for all contractors, Waste transfer notes for all waste movements, Hazardous waste consignment notes, Site waste management plan maintained, Periodic waste audit records, No unlicensed waste deposits

Unnamed: 1: Contaminated Land (England) Regulations 2006 / EPA 1990 — Contaminated Land
Unnamed: 2: Environment Agency / Local Authority Environmental Health
Unnamed: 4: Sites with known or suspected contamination require: Phase 1 Environmental Site Investigation (desk study), Phase 2 site investigation (intrusive surveys) where risk identified, Remediation Strategy, and validation report post-remediation. Planning permission will typically include conditions requiring all phases. Occupiers of contaminated land may have liability under Part IIA EPA 1990.
Unnamed: 5: Planning enforcement; stop work; civil liability for contamination; LA remediation notice; reputational damage; Homes England grant condition breach
Unnamed: 6: Site acquisition / Pre-application on brownfield land / Planning condition requiring investigation
Unnamed: 7: Phase 1 investigation trigger on brownfield site identification, Phase 2 trigger on Phase 1 findings, remediation validation sign-off before occupation gate
Unnamed: 8: Environmental Consultant, Environment Agency, LA Environmental Health, Planning Authority, Principal Contractor
Unnamed: 9: Commission Phase 1 Environmental Site Investigation (desk study) → If risk identified: commission Phase 2 intrusive survey → Develop Remediation Strategy → Submit to LPA and EA for approval → Implement remediation works → Validate remediation → Submit validation report to LPA → Receive planning condition discharge confirmation → File all reports in project document control
Unnamed: 10: Phase 1 ESI report on file, Phase 2 site investigation report (if required), Remediation Strategy approved by LPA/EA, Remediation completion evidence, Validation report submitted, Planning condition discharge confirmation

Unnamed: 1: New Roads and Street Works Act 1991 — Utility Surveys & Diversions
Unnamed: 4: Before any works commence near existing utility infrastructure, utility records must be obtained, ground penetrating radar surveys conducted where required, and any utility diversions planned and executed. Utility companies must be consulted and diversion works instructed with adequate lead time. Cat & Genny surveys required before any excavation.
Unnamed: 5: Damage to utilities: criminal prosecution, unlimited fines, supply disruption, civil liability, significant programme delay and cost
Unnamed: 6: Pre-construction — before any excavation works or works in proximity to known utility routes
Unnamed: 7: Utility search and survey reminder at pre-construction stage, Cat & Genny mandatory check gate before excavation
Unnamed: 8: Utility Companies (gas, electric, water, telecoms, drainage), Principal Contractor, Structural Engineer, Site Manager
Unnamed: 9: Obtain statutory utility records from all utilities → Review records and identify conflicts with proposed works → Commission GPR survey where required → Consult utility companies for diversions required → Agree diversion routes and timescales → Instruct diversions with adequate lead time (typically 6-12 weeks for major utilities) → Confirm diversions complete before works begin → Cat & Genny before every excavation
Unnamed: 10: Statutory utility records obtained from all companies, GPR survey report (where required), Utility diversion design approved, Diversion works completion certificates, Cat & Genny records for all excavations

Unnamed: 1: New Roads and Street Works Act 1991 — NRSWA Permits & Street Works
Unnamed: 4: Works in the public highway require NRSWA permits from the highway authority before works begin. Different permit types: permit notice for minor works, standard notice for standard works, major works requiring traffic regulation orders. Street works must be carried out by NRSWA-accredited operatives and supervisors. Reinstatement must meet HAUC specification.
Unnamed: 5: Fixed penalty notices; stop work; fines; highway authority remedial works at contractor's cost; future restrictions on working in highway
Unnamed: 6: Any works in public highway — before works commence
Unnamed: 7: NRSWA permit application reminder (timing varies: 1 day minor, 3 days standard, 10 days major), permit expiry alert, reinstatement completion reminder
Unnamed: 8: Principal Contractor, NRSWA-accredited operatives and supervisors, Highway Authority, Traffic Management Contractor
Unnamed: 9: Determine permit type required → Apply for NRSWA permit with required notice period → Traffic management plan → Carry out works using NRSWA-accredited operatives → Complete reinstatement to HAUC specification → Notify highway authority of completion → Defects liability period monitoring (2 years for permanent reinstatement)
Unnamed: 10: NRSWA permits obtained for all highway works, Traffic management plans approved, NRSWA-accredited operative records, Reinstatement inspection records, Highway authority completion notices, Defects liability monitoring records

Unnamed: 1: New Roads and Street Works Act 1991 / Individual Connection Agreements — Utility Connections & Adoption
Unnamed: 2: Utility Companies / Highway Authority / OFWAT / Ofgem
Unnamed: 4: Each utility connection (gas, electricity, water, drainage, telecoms) must be applied for, designed, inspected and adopted separately. Connection to the public sewer requires Section 104 agreement (adoption) with the sewerage undertaker. New roads may require Section 38 adoption agreement. All connections must be inspected and adopted before occupation.
Unnamed: 5: Occupation without adopted utilities: building occupation restricted; mortgage and sale blocked; Homes England grant condition breach; ongoing liability for unadopted infrastructure
Unnamed: 6: Pre-construction (applications) / Practical completion (adoption inspections) / Before occupation
Unnamed: 7: Connection application deadline alert by utility type, S104 adoption inspection scheduling, S38 roads adoption tracker, occupation gate blocked until all connections adopted
Unnamed: 8: Utility Companies, Sewerage Undertaker, Highway Authority, Homes England, Solicitors, Finance
Unnamed: 9: Apply for all utility connections with adequate lead time → Design connections to utility company specification → Section 104 agreement with sewerage undertaker for sewer adoption → Section 38 agreement with highway authority for roads adoption → Coordinate all connections during construction → Inspection and adoption for each utility at practical completion → Adopt all utilities before occupation → Register connections on asset register
Unnamed: 10: Connection applications submitted for all utilities, S104 agreement executed with sewerage undertaker, S38 agreement executed with highway authority, Connection inspection and adoption records, Utility company adoption certificates, Asset register updated with all connections

Unnamed: 1: Grid Connection — G98/G99 DNO Notification (ENA Engineering Recommendations)
Unnamed: 4: Solar PV and battery storage must be notified to and approved by the local DNO. G98: micro-generation up to 3.68kW per phase — notify DNO 28 days before energisation. G99: larger systems — formal DNO application, protection settings review and commissioning test required before energisation. MCS certification of installer required.
Unnamed: 5: DNO can disconnect unlawful connections; grid instability risk; MCS warranty void; no Smart Export Guarantee payments
Unnamed: 6: Solar PV or battery storage included in project scope / Pre-installation
Unnamed: 7: G98 28-day notification countdown, G99 DNO application tracker, MCS certificate verification, commissioning test reminder
Unnamed: 8: MCS-certified Installer, DNO, Project Manager, Electrical Engineer, Asset Management
Unnamed: 9: Determine G98 or G99 threshold → G98: notify DNO 28 days before energisation → G99: submit formal DNO application → Confirm protection settings → Commission with MCS-certified installer → Obtain MCS certificate → Commissioning test → Register for Smart Export Guarantee → Update asset register
Unnamed: 10: G98 notification acknowledgement or G99 DNO approval, Protection settings confirmed, MCS certificates per installation, Commissioning test certificates, SEG registration confirmation, Asset register updated

Unnamed: 1: NHBC Standards / Warranty Provider Registration — Scheme Registration
Unnamed: 4: New residential development must be registered with an approved warranty provider before construction commences. NHBC Buildmark (or equivalent) must be registered per plot. Registration triggers technical audit programme and provides the warranty certificate required for mortgage lending and sale. Without warranty registration, units cannot be mortgaged or sold.
Unnamed: 5: Plots cannot be mortgaged or sold without warranty certificate; Homes England grant condition breach; lender requirements breached; reputational damage
Unnamed: 6: Before construction commences / At point of plot registration
Unnamed: 7: Warranty registration deadline alert before site start, per-plot registration completion tracker
Unnamed: 8: Developer, NHBC/Warranty Provider, Solicitors, Homes England, Mortgage Lenders
Unnamed: 9: Select approved warranty provider → Register scheme with warranty provider → Register each plot individually → Pay registration fees → Confirm technical auditor appointment → Brief site management team on warranty requirements → Display NHBC/warranty provider site board → Maintain registration throughout construction

Unnamed: 1: NHBC Standards — Staged Technical Audits During Construction
Unnamed: 4: The warranty provider conducts staged technical audits throughout construction at key milestones: foundations, structural frame, envelope, internal works, pre-completion. The site team must prepare for each audit, resolve any audit findings promptly, and maintain quality standards throughout. Failed audits or unresolved issues can delay completion certificates.
Unnamed: 5: Warranty certificate refused; mortgage lending blocked; plots unsellable; significant rework costs; Homes England grant condition breach
Unnamed: 6: Each construction milestone as defined by warranty provider inspection programme
Unnamed: 7: Technical audit scheduling alerts, audit finding response deadline tracker, unresolved finding escalation flag
Unnamed: 8: Site Manager, Principal Contractor, NHBC/Warranty Inspector, Clerk of Works (where appointed), QA Team
Unnamed: 9: Confirm audit schedule with warranty provider at scheme start → Prepare for each audit: check readiness of relevant works → Attend audit with site manager → Receive and log audit findings → Address all findings within agreed timescale → Obtain sign-off from warranty provider on each stage → Escalate persistent issues to senior management → Maintain audit records throughout construction
Unnamed: 10: Full audit schedule on file, All staged audit records with findings, Finding response and close-out records, Stage sign-off records from warranty provider, No outstanding unresolved findings at completion

Unnamed: 1: NHBC Standards — Pre-Completion Snagging
Unnamed: 4: Before practical completion and handover, a comprehensive pre-completion inspection must be conducted. All defects, snags and quality issues must be identified, logged, rectified and signed off before the unit is handed over. A zero-defect (or agreed minimal defect) position should be achieved before handover to the purchaser or RP.
Unnamed: 5: Financial loss from post-completion claims; reputational damage; Housing Ombudsman complaints if defects not resolved; NHBC warranty claims; contractual disputes
Unnamed: 6: Before practical completion / Before handover to purchaser or Registered Provider
Unnamed: 7: Pre-completion snagging inspection scheduling alert, snagging list completion tracker, zero-defect sign-off gate before handover
Unnamed: 8: QA Team, Site Manager, Principal Contractor, Clerk of Works, Purchaser/RP representative, NHBC Inspector
Unnamed: 9: Programme pre-completion inspection 4-6 weeks before practical completion → Conduct thorough internal and external snagging inspection → Log all defects with location and description → Issue snagging schedule to contractor with completion deadlines → Inspect completed snags → Re-inspect failed items → Achieve agreed standard before handover → Joint handover inspection with purchaser/RP → Sign off snagging list at handover
Unnamed: 10: Pre-completion snagging inspection records, Snagging schedules with all items logged, Completion evidence for all snagging items, Joint handover inspection records, Signed snagging close-out confirmation from purchaser/RP, Zero outstanding defects at handover

Unnamed: 1: NHBC Buildmark Warranty — Handover Pack & Warranty Period Management
Unnamed: 4: At handover, each plot must receive: NHBC Buildmark warranty certificate, Homeowner's Manual (O&M information), energy performance certificate, relevant test certificates, building control completion certificate. During the builder's warranty period (typically 2 years), defects arising from builder's workmanship must be rectified. NHBC assumes responsibility for structural defects from years 3-10.
Unnamed: 5: Mortgage lender requirements breached without warranty certificate; NHBC claim for unresolved builder defects; Ombudsman complaint; reputational damage
Unnamed: 6: Practical completion / Handover to purchaser or RP / During 2-year builder warranty period
