Unnamed: 9: Compile complete handover pack for each plot (warranty certificate, O&M manual, EPC, test certificates, completion certificate) → Hand over to purchaser/RP with receipt confirmation → Establish customer care / defect reporting process → Respond to defect reports within agreed SLA during 2-year builder warranty period → Instruct contractor to rectify builder defects → Sign off completed warranty works → Record all warranty claims and outcomes → At year 2: confirm handover to NHBC structural warranty period
Unnamed: 10: Handover packs issued with receipt confirmation for all plots, Warranty certificates registered and issued, O&M manuals issued, Defect report log with response and completion records, All builder defects rectified within warranty period, Year 2 handover to NHBC structural warranty confirmed

Unnamed: 1: Social Housing (Regulation) Act 2023 — Consumer Standards Annual Self-Assessment
Unnamed: 4: Registered Providers must conduct and document an annual self-assessment against all four Consumer Standards: Safety & Quality, Transparency Influence & Accountability, Neighbourhood & Community, and Tenancy. The self-assessment must be Board-approved, identify gaps and service improvement plans, and be retained for RSH inspection. RSH may request self-assessment at any time.
Unnamed: 5: RSH regulatory notice; improvement plan; special measures; public censure; unlimited enforcement powers
Unnamed: 6: Annual regulatory cycle — typically aligned to financial year end / RSH inspection request
Unnamed: 7: Annual self-assessment reminder (6 months before year end), gap analysis tracker, Board sign-off workflow
Unnamed: 8: CEO/Board, Housing Management, Asset Management, Tenant Engagement team, Equalities Officer, RSH
Unnamed: 9: Schedule annual self-assessment process → Assign leads for each Consumer Standard → Gather evidence from all service areas → Assess compliance against each standard indicator → Identify gaps and service failures → Develop improvement plan for each gap → Board review and sign-off → File self-assessment and evidence pack → Prepare for RSH inspection using self-assessment
Unnamed: 10: Annual self-assessment completed for all four Consumer Standards, Evidence pack compiled per standard, Gaps and improvement plan documented, Board sign-off minutes, Self-assessment and evidence retained for RSH inspection

Unnamed: 1: Social Housing (Regulation) Act 2023 — Tenant Satisfaction Measures (TSM) Collection & Submission
Unnamed: 4: Registered Providers must collect Tenant Satisfaction Measure data across 22 measures (12 perception measures from tenant survey, 10 management measures from operational data) and submit annual TSM return to RSH by June each year. TSM results must be published to residents in an accessible format. Survey methodology must comply with RSH requirements.
Unnamed: 5: RSH regulatory notice; public censure; increased scrutiny; regulatory judgement downgrade
Unnamed: 6: Annual TSM survey period (typically October-March) / RSH submission deadline: June each year / Resident publication: within 12 months of data period
Unnamed: 7: TSM survey period launch reminder, June submission deadline countdown (90/30/7 days), resident publication reminder after submission

Unnamed: 10: TSM survey completed to RSH methodology requirements, All 22 TSM data points collected and validated, TSM return submitted to RSH by June deadline (submission confirmation), Results published to residents in accessible format, Board presentation of results, Improvement plan for any underperforming measures

Unnamed: 1: Social Housing (Regulation) Act 2023 — RSH Proactive Inspection Readiness
Unnamed: 4: RSH now conducts proactive consumer inspections of all registered providers on a rolling programme. Providers must be inspection-ready at all times. RSH inspectors will assess: consumer standard compliance, safety and quality of stock, resident engagement, complaints handling, and governance. RSH can give as little as 28 days notice of an inspection.

Unnamed: 6: Ongoing — RSH can request inspection at any time with 28 days notice / After significant consumer complaint / After regulatory engagement

Unnamed: 10: Standing inspection readiness pack maintained and current, Quarterly evidence pack updates evidenced, Annual mock inspection completed, Board briefing records, Ability to respond to 28-day inspection notice with complete evidence

Unnamed: 1: RSH Governance & Financial Viability Standard — Annual Accounts Submission
Unnamed: 4: Registered Providers must submit statutory accounts to RSH annually and meet the Governance & Financial Viability Standard independently of Consumer Standards. The G&FV Standard requires: effective Board governance, robust risk management, viable business plan with stress-testing, and annual accounts submitted on time. RSH grades providers on G&FV (G1/G2/G3/G4 and V1/V2/V3/V4).
Unnamed: 5: RSH regulatory notice; special measures; deregistration; Board governance intervention by RSH; unlimited enforcement action
Unnamed: 6: Annual accounts submission deadline / RSH regulatory engagement

Unnamed: 10: Annual accounts submitted to RSH on time, External audit sign-off, Board approval minutes, G&FV self-assessment signed off by Board, G&FV self-assessment submitted to RSH, RSH engagement records, G&FV grade maintained

Unnamed: 1: RSH Governance & Financial Viability Standard — Material Change Notification
Unnamed: 4: Registered Providers must notify RSH of any material change to their governance, financial viability or operations within one working day for serious events. Material events include: significant financial distress, Board restructuring, merger/acquisition, loss of key contracts, significant legal proceedings, fraud, or any event that could affect the RP's ability to deliver its functions. Annual stress-testing of business plan required.
Unnamed: 5: RSH enforcement action for failure to notify material change; regulatory investigation; special measures

Unnamed: 1: RSH Transparency Standard — Annual Publication & Resident Engagement
Unnamed: 4: The Transparency, Influence & Accountability Standard requires RPs to: publish prescribed information about their services, provide residents with meaningful opportunities to influence decisions, maintain Board accountability structures, and publish a Register of Board Member Interests. Annual scrutiny panel or equivalent required. Board member Register of Interests must be published.
Unnamed: 5: RSH regulatory notice; improvement plan; public censure; Consumer Standards inspection finding

Unnamed: 1: Decent Homes Standard / RSH Consumer Standards — Stock Condition Survey Programme
Unnamed: 4: All social landlords must conduct a comprehensive stock condition survey of their entire housing portfolio on a rolling programme (typically 5-yearly). The survey must assess all components against the Decent Homes Standard criteria: free from Category 1 HHSRS hazards, in reasonable repair, with reasonably modern facilities and adequate thermal comfort. Survey data must be held on an asset management system and kept current.
Unnamed: 5: RSH Consumer Standards breach (Safety & Quality); regulatory notice; inability to demonstrate compliance; uninformed investment programme

Unnamed: 1: Housing Act 2004 / HHSRS — Hazard Assessment & Remediation
Unnamed: 4: Landlords must assess all properties using the Housing Health and Safety Rating System (HHSRS) across 29 hazard profiles. Category 1 hazards (score >1000) require mandatory remediation. Category 2 hazards should be addressed. Local Authorities have enforcement powers. Tenant complaints should trigger HHSRS inspection.
Unnamed: 5: Improvement notices; prohibition orders; emergency remediation; civil penalties up to £30,000; LA enforcement costs recovered from landlord
Unnamed: 6: Tenant complaint / Routine property inspection / LA inspection request / Pre-tenancy check

Unnamed: 1: Decent Homes Standard — Non-Decent Homes Remediation Programme
Unnamed: 4: All social homes identified as non-decent must be brought to the Decent Homes Standard through a costed, funded and programmed remediation programme. The programme must address: Category 1 HHSRS hazards, disrepair, kitchen/bathroom standards (30-year replacement criterion), and thermal comfort (EPC Band D minimum, moving to Band C by 2030). Progress must be reported to RSH annually.
Unnamed: 5: RSH regulatory notice; special measures; government intervention; reputational harm; tenant disrepair claims

Unnamed: 1: MEES / Decent Homes Uplift — EPC Portfolio Assessment & Band C Trajectory
Unnamed: 4: All rented properties must have minimum EPC Band E. Social housing target is Band C by 2030 (Decent Homes uplift). Valid EPC required at commencement of tenancy and on re-let. Sub-Band E properties cannot be lawfully re-let. Full EPC portfolio assessment required to establish Band C gap and investment programme.

Unnamed: 1: RSH Consumer Standards / DLUHC — Annual Regulatory Return
Unnamed: 4: Registered Providers must submit an annual regulatory return to RSH covering: stock data, non-decent homes, safety compliance (gas, fire, electrical, asbestos, water, lift safety), complaints, TSMs, and financial data. The return must be accurate and submitted on time. Local Authority landlords must submit the Local Authority Data Return (LADR). Data quality is assessed by RSH.

Unnamed: 1: Gas Safety (Installation and Use) Regulations 1998 — Annual Gas Safety Certificate
Unnamed: 4: Landlords must ensure an annual gas safety check by Gas Safe registered engineer for all gas appliances, flues and fittings in rented properties. Gas Safety Certificate (GSC) must be issued, kept for 2 years, provided to existing tenants within 28 days, and to new tenants before occupation. All gas appliances must be maintained in safe working order throughout.

Unnamed: 10: Valid GSC on file for every gas property, No properties with expired GSC, Tenant issuance evidence for all certificates, Void property isolation records, 100% compliance register

Unnamed: 1: Gas Safety (Installation and Use) Regulations 1998 — Void Property Gas Management
Unnamed: 4: Void (empty) properties with gas appliances require specific management: gas supply must be isolated on vacation, and gas appliances must not be recommissioned until a valid gas safety check has been completed by a Gas Safe registered engineer before the next tenancy. Gas must not be restored to a void without a current GSC in place.
Unnamed: 5: Criminal prosecution if gas restored without valid GSC; tenant harm; civil liability; unlimited fines
Unnamed: 6: Property becoming void / Before re-let of any gas property
Unnamed: 7: Void property gas isolation check on tenancy end confirmation, pre-let GSC completion gate — property cannot be re-let without current GSC

Unnamed: 10: Gas isolation records for all void properties, Pre-let GSC completed before re-let for all gas properties, New GSC filed on compliance register, No gas properties re-let without current GSC

Unnamed: 1: Electrical Safety Standards — EICR 5-Yearly Programme
Unnamed: 4: Electrical installations must be inspected by a Part P/NICEIC qualified electrician every 5 years (or at change of tenancy). An Electrical Installation Condition Report (EICR) must be issued. The EICR classifies findings: C1 (danger present — immediate action required), C2 (potentially dangerous — urgent action), C3 (improvement recommended).
Unnamed: 5: RSH Consumer Standards breach (Safety & Quality); civil penalty up to £30,000; civil liability if electrical fault causes harm
Unnamed: 6: 5-year EICR renewal date per property / New tenancy commencement / Electrical works completed

Unnamed: 10: Valid EICR on file for every property, No expired EICRs, Programme schedule for all properties, EICR records on compliance register

Unnamed: 1: Electrical Safety Standards — EICR C1 & C2 Remediation
Unnamed: 4: C1 findings (danger present) require immediate remediation before the property can be safely occupied. C2 findings (potentially dangerous) must be remediated within 90 days. All C1 and C2 works must be completed by a qualified electrician, with a follow-up inspection confirming the installation is now satisfactory. Failure to remediate C1/C2 findings is a regulatory and safety risk.
Unnamed: 5: RSH Consumer Standards breach; civil claims; criminal liability if C1 hazard causes harm; property cannot be occupied with unresolved C1 finding
Unnamed: 6: On receipt of EICR identifying C1 or C2 findings

Unnamed: 10: C1 emergency works orders raised immediately, C1 remediation completion evidence before occupation, C2 works orders within 7 days, C2 remediation completion within 90 days, Post-remediation inspection confirming satisfactory outcome, Updated compliance register

Unnamed: 1: Legionella — L8 ACOP / COSHH 2002 — Risk Assessment & Written Control Scheme
Unnamed: 4: Duty holders must conduct a Legionella risk assessment of all domestic hot and cold water systems. A written control scheme must be developed. Risk assessment must be reviewed every 2 years or on significant change. Communal systems (cooling towers, spa pools, calorifiers) require more intensive management.
Unnamed: 5: Criminal prosecution, unlimited fines, imprisonment; civil claims if Legionnaires' disease results (notifiable disease)
Unnamed: 6: New property / New tenancy / Post-void / Significant change to water system / 2-year review cycle
Unnamed: 7: Risk assessment expiry alert (2 years), new property trigger on acquisition, significant water system change trigger

Unnamed: 9: Commission Legionella risk assessment for all domestic water systems → Develop written control scheme per system type → Implement routine monitoring programme → Set review date (maximum 2 years) → Enhanced assessment for communal systems → Maintain 5-year records → File risk assessments on compliance register
Unnamed: 10: Legionella risk assessment on file per property/system type, Written control scheme documented, 5-year records maintained, Review dates set and tracked, No Legionella-related incidents or complaints

Unnamed: 1: Legionella — L8 ACOP — Ongoing Monitoring, Flushing & Records
Unnamed: 4: Following risk assessment, the written control scheme must be implemented: monthly temperature checks and flushing of all infrequently used outlets (cold water <20°C within 2 minutes, hot water >50°C within 1 minute at outlet), documentation of all monitoring activities, and prompt remedial action if temperatures are out of range. Records must be maintained for 5 years.
Unnamed: 5: Criminal prosecution; civil claims; reputational damage; HSE enforcement notice
Unnamed: 6: Monthly — ongoing throughout occupation of all properties
Unnamed: 7: Monthly flushing and temperature check reminder per property type, out-of-range temperature escalation alert, 5-year record retention reminder

Unnamed: 10: Monthly monitoring records for all properties and communal systems, Temperature check logs, Out-of-range escalation records with remedial actions, 5-year record retention confirmed, Annual monitoring programme review

Unnamed: 1: LOLER 1998 — Written Scheme of Examination & 6-Monthly Passenger Lift Examination
Unnamed: 4: All lifting equipment (passenger lifts, service lifts, hoists, MEWPs, cranes) must be subject to thorough examination by a competent person at defined intervals. Passenger lifts: every 6 months. Written scheme of examination required for all lifting equipment. Examination reports must be retained. Defects posing immediate danger must be reported to HSE.
Unnamed: 5: HSE Improvement/Prohibition Notices, unlimited fines, prosecution, civil liability for injury

Unnamed: 10: Written scheme of examination in place, 6-monthly examination certificates for all passenger lifts, Examination records for all lifting equipment, Remedial actions completed and evidenced, Immediate risk defects reported to HSE, No overdue examinations

Unnamed: 1: MEES / Decent Homes — EPC Portfolio Assessment & Sub-Band E Compliance
Unnamed: 4: All rented properties must have a valid EPC and meet minimum Band E. Sub-Band E properties cannot be lawfully re-let. A full portfolio EPC audit must identify all properties below Band E for immediate action and all properties below Band C for the 2030 programme. EPC certificates expire after 10 years and must be renewed.
Unnamed: 5: Civil penalties up to £5,000 per property per breach; sub-Band E lettings block; RSH Consumer Standards breach; reputational damage
Unnamed: 6: Tenancy commencement / Re-let / EPC expiry / Annual DHS review
Unnamed: 7: EPC expiry alert (90/30/7 days), sub-Band E lettings block flag on property database

Unnamed: 9: Audit entire portfolio for valid EPCs → Commission assessments for expired or missing EPCs → Identify all sub-Band E properties → Block sub-Band E properties from re-letting → Commission emergency retrofit works for sub-Band E properties → Obtain post-works EPC → Confirm Band E minimum achieved → Unblock from re-letting → Update compliance register
Unnamed: 10: Valid EPC for all properties on asset register, Sub-Band E properties identified and blocked from re-letting, Emergency retrofit for sub-Band E properties completed, Post-works EPC confirming Band E minimum, Compliance register 100% complete

Unnamed: 1: MEES / Decent Homes Uplift — Band C Gap Analysis & 2030 Trajectory
Unnamed: 4: All social housing must achieve EPC Band C by 2030 (Decent Homes uplift). A comprehensive Band C gap analysis must establish the investment required, the programme of works, and the funding strategy. Annual progress must be tracked and reported to RSH. Properties must be prioritised by current EPC rating, tenure, and retrofit feasibility.
Unnamed: 5: RSH regulatory concern for insufficient progress toward Band C target; reputational harm; loss of funding eligibility
Unnamed: 6: Annual planning cycle / RSH regulatory engagement / Homes England funding application
Unnamed: 7: Band C gap analysis dashboard, annual trajectory update reminder, funding round opening alerts (SHDF, ECO4)

Unnamed: 9: Commission Band C gap analysis for all stock → Categorise properties by current EPC band (B,C,D,E,F,G) → Calculate investment required to reach Band C → Develop 2030 delivery programme with annual milestones → Identify funding sources (SHDF, ECO4, HUG2, own capital) → Submit funding applications → Report annual progress to RSH and Board → Update trajectory as works completed

Unnamed: 1: Landlord & Tenant Act 1985 Section 20 — Notice of Intention Stage
Unnamed: 4: The S20 process begins when qualifying works exceed £250 per leaseholder (or qualifying long-term agreements exceed £100 per leaseholder per year). Stage 1: serve Notice of Intention on all leaseholders describing proposed works/agreement, inviting observations within 30 days, and inviting nomination of contractors to provide estimates.
Unnamed: 5: Cost recovery capped at £250 per leaseholder if consultation not followed; FTT determination; leaseholder challenge
Unnamed: 6: Works estimated to exceed £250 per leaseholder / Before inviting any tenders or entering any agreement

Unnamed: 10: Notice of Intention served on all leaseholders with proof of service, 30-day observation period logged, All observations received and recorded, Contractor nominations considered and documented

Unnamed: 1: Landlord & Tenant Act 1985 Section 20 — Estimates & Notice of Proposal Stage
Unnamed: 4: After the observation period, obtain at least 2 estimates for the works. If any leaseholder nominated a contractor, an estimate must be obtained from that contractor. Issue Notice of Proposal to all leaseholders with the estimates (at least 2), invite observations within 30 days. If not accepting the lowest estimate, reasons must be given.
Unnamed: 5: Cost recovery capped at £250 per leaseholder if fewer than 2 estimates; FTT challenge if nominated contractor's estimate not obtained

Unnamed: 7: Estimate collection deadline tracker, Notice of Proposal preparation reminder, 30-day observation period countdown

Unnamed: 10: Minimum 2 estimates obtained (including nominated if applicable), Notice of Proposal served on all leaseholders with proof of service, 30-day observation period logged, All observations received and recorded, Reasons for not accepting lowest estimate documented (if applicable)

Unnamed: 1: Landlord & Tenant Act 1985 Section 20 — Notice to Proceed, Contract Award & Final Demand
Unnamed: 4: After the second observation period, award the contract to the chosen contractor. Issue Notice to Proceed to all leaseholders before works commence. Following completion of works, issue the final service charge demand which must include: the total cost, each leaseholder's contribution, and a statement confirming S20 consultation was carried out.

Unnamed: 10: Contract award with reasons documented, Notice to Proceed served on all leaseholders before works, Final service charge demand issued to all leaseholders, S20 compliance statement included in demand, Complete S20 correspondence file retained

Unnamed: 1: Landlord & Tenant Act 1985 Sections 18–30 — Annual Service Charge Accounts
Unnamed: 4: Service charges must be reasonably incurred, works/services must be of a reasonable standard, and charges must be calculated in accordance with the lease. Annual service charge accounts must be prepared within 6 months of the service charge year end and issued to all leaseholders.

Unnamed: 1: Landlord & Tenant Act 1985 Section 21 — Summary of Rights & Document Inspection
Unnamed: 4: Leaseholders must be provided with a Summary of Rights and Obligations with every demand for service charges. On request, leaseholders are entitled to inspect all documents, receipts, and accounts supporting service charge demands within 21 days of request. Failure to comply allows leaseholder to withhold service charges.

Unnamed: 4: Ground rent on new regulated leases must be zero (peppercorn) from 30 June 2022. Prohibited from charging ground rent on existing leases where extended under the statutory route after 30 June 2022. Annual ground rent demands on affected leases are unlawful. Full portfolio audit required.
Unnamed: 5: Civil penalties up to £30,000 per breach; Trading Standards enforcement; reputational damage
Unnamed: 6: New lease granted / Statutory lease extension / Ground rent review / Annual portfolio audit

Unnamed: 4: Landlord must keep in repair the structure and exterior of the dwelling, and all installations for water, gas, electricity, sanitation, and space and water heating. Repairs must be completed within reasonable timescales depending on urgency. Housing Disrepair Protocol applies if tenant issues formal legal claim.

Unnamed: 1: Landlord & Tenant Act 1985 Section 10A — Awaab's Law: Case Triage & Emergency Response
Unnamed: 4: On receipt of any damp or mould report: severity must be assessed within 24 hours. If emergency hazard: repair must be instructed within 24 hours. If non-emergency: hazard investigation must be completed within 14 days. Repair works must commence within 7 days of completed investigation. All timescales are statutory and cannot be waived.

Unnamed: 10: Timestamped case log from first contact, 24-hour severity assessment record, Emergency repair instruction within 24 hours (if applicable), Survey report within 14 days, Works order within 7 days of completed investigation, Remedial works completion evidence, Tenant satisfaction confirmation, Photographic before/after evidence

Unnamed: 4: Personal data breaches must be detected and assessed promptly. Where a breach is likely to result in a risk to individuals, ICO must be notified within 72 hours of becoming aware. Where risk is high, affected individuals must also be notified. Breach log must be maintained for all breaches (notifiable and non-notifiable). Breach response plan must be in place.

Unnamed: 10: Breach reporting training for all staff, Breach log maintained for all incidents, ICO notification within 72 hours (where required) with submission reference, Individual notifications (where high risk), Remedial measures implemented and evidenced, Annual breach trend analysis

Unnamed: 4: The Golden Thread is a digital, structured and version-controlled record of all building safety information for HRBs. Unnamed: 1: Public Services (Social Value) Act 2012 — Social Value at Tender Stage
Unnamed: 4: Contracting authorities must consider how contracts can improve economic, social, and environmental well-being in the area before commencing procurement. Social value must be specified in tender documents with measurable outcomes and typically weighted at 10% of tender evaluation. Bidders must demonstrate how they will deliver social value outcomes.

Unnamed: 1: Public Services (Social Value) Act 2012 — Contract Delivery & Evidence Collection
Unnamed: 4: Throughout the contract, the contractor must deliver social value commitments and provide evidence quarterly. The contract manager must monitor delivery, validate evidence, and escalate under-delivery. Social value outputs typically include: local employment, apprenticeships, supply chain spend in the area, volunteer hours, community engagement, and environmental improvements.
Unnamed: 5: Contract termination; financial penalty; loss of future procurement opportunities; reputational damage with contracting authority
Unnamed: 6: Quarterly contract reporting milestones / Annual contract review / Contract completion
Unnamed: 7: Quarterly social value evidence submission reminder, KPI performance dashboard, under-delivery escalation alert
Unnamed: 8: Contract Manager, Contractor, Local businesses, Employment support organisations, Finance, Communications
Unnamed: 9: Set up social value monitoring framework with contractor → Collect quarterly evidence from contractor → Validate evidence against agreed methodology → Log all outputs on social value tracker → Identify any under-delivery and agree recovery plan → Quarterly reporting to client/Board → Annual social value impact calculation → Respond to evidence audit requests
Unnamed: 10: Social value monitoring framework established, Quarterly evidence reports received from contractor, Evidence validated and logged, Annual social value impact calculated and verified, No unresolved under-delivery situations

Unnamed: 1: Public Services (Social Value) Act 2012 — Annual Social Value Impact Report
Unnamed: 4: An annual social value impact report must be produced, submitted to the contracting authority, and published to demonstrate outcomes. The report must cover all contracted outputs, compare against commitments, calculate financial proxy values (HACT Social Value Bank or equivalent), and identify learning for future contracts. Report must be Board-approved.
Unnamed: 5: Loss of future procurement opportunities; reputational damage; non-compliance with contract reporting requirement
Unnamed: 6: Annual contract reporting deadline / Contract close-out
Unnamed: 7: Annual impact report deadline reminder, Board sign-off workflow, publication confirmation
Unnamed: 8: Contract Manager, Finance, Communications, Board, Contracting Authority, Community stakeholders
Unnamed: 9: Compile all quarterly evidence → Calculate annual social value outputs against commitments → Apply financial proxy values (HACT or equivalent) → Draft annual social value impact report → Board review and approval → Submit to contracting authority by deadline → Publish on website/stakeholder channels → Communicate impact to local communities → Use findings to inform future social value requirements
Unnamed: 10: Annual social value impact report completed with all outputs evidenced, Financial proxy values calculated, Board approval minutes, Report submitted to contracting authority by deadline, Report published and communicated to stakeholders

Unnamed: 1: Party Wall etc. Act 1996 — Notice Service
Unnamed: 4: Owners must serve formal notice on adjoining owners before: building on or at the boundary (Line of Junction Notice — 1 month notice), carrying out works to a shared party wall (Party Structure Notice — 2 months notice), or excavating within 3m or 6m of an adjoining building (3m/6m Notice — 1 month notice). Works cannot commence until the notice period has elapsed and either consent is received or an Award is made.
Unnamed: 5: Injunction to halt works; civil damages from adjoining owners; court orders; programme delay; cost overruns
Unnamed: 6: Pre-construction design stage / When works affecting party walls or boundaries identified
Unnamed: 7: Party wall notice identification at design stage, notice service deadline alert (2 months before planned start for party structure works)
Unnamed: 8: Party Wall Surveyor, Project Manager, Legal, Adjoining property owners, Principal Contractor, Design team
Unnamed: 9: Identify all works affecting party walls, boundaries or excavations near adjoining structures → Determine notice type(s) required (Party Structure, Line of Junction, 3/6m excavation) → Identify all adjoining owners and their service addresses → Serve appropriate notices by recorded delivery or personal service → Log service dates and notice periods → Await response (14-day consent or dissent period for party structure notices)
Unnamed: 10: All works requiring notice identified at design stage, Correct notice type(s) identified, All adjoining owners identified with service addresses, Notices served with proof of service (recorded delivery receipts), Notice service dates logged, 14-day response period monitored

Unnamed: 1: Party Wall etc. Act 1996 — Surveyor Appointment & Party Wall Award
Unnamed: 4: If an adjoining owner dissents or fails to respond, surveyors must be appointed. Either an agreed surveyor for both parties, or each party appoints their own surveyor with a third surveyor nominated. Surveyors must agree a Party Wall Award setting out: the works to be carried out, working hours, protection measures, and the right of access. The Award is legally binding.
Unnamed: 5: Works cannot commence on party wall until Award is made; injunction if works proceed without Award; surveyor fees become dispute
Unnamed: 6: On receipt of dissent or deemed dissent (no response within 14 days)
Unnamed: 7: Dissent/no-response flag triggering surveyor appointment requirement, Award agreement progress tracker
Unnamed: 8: Party Wall Surveyor (Building Owner's), Party Wall Surveyor (Adjoining Owner's), Third Surveyor (if required), Legal, Project Manager
Unnamed: 9: On dissent: appoint party wall surveyor for building owner → Adjoining owner appoints their surveyor (or agreed surveyor used) → Nominate third surveyor in advance → Surveyors inspect and agree scope → Draft Party Wall Award → Both surveyors agree and sign Award → Serve Award on both owners → Commission schedule of condition of adjoining property → Confirm Award received and works can proceed
Unnamed: 10: Dissent recorded and surveyor appointment evidence, Party Wall Award signed by relevant surveyors, Award served on both owners with proof, Schedule of condition of adjoining property commissioned and filed, Works authorised under Award

Unnamed: 1: Party Wall etc. Act 1996 — Post-Completion Inspection
Unnamed: 4: After completion of party wall works, a post-completion inspection should be carried out by the surveyors to compare the condition of the adjoining property with the schedule of condition prepared before works. Any damage caused by the works must be made good by the building owner. Records of the inspection must be retained.
Unnamed: 5: Civil claim for damage; surveyor-ordered remedial works; dispute over causation without post-works inspection
Unnamed: 6: On completion of all party wall works
Unnamed: 7: Post-completion inspection scheduling reminder on works completion, damage claim response tracker
Unnamed: 8: Party Wall Surveyor, Adjoining Owner, Project Manager, Contractor
Unnamed: 9: Programme post-completion inspection with party wall surveyor → Inspect adjoining property → Compare condition with pre-works schedule of condition → Identify any damage attributable to works → Agree remedial works with adjoining owner → Instruct contractor to carry out remedial works → Final sign-off from adjoining owner → File post-completion inspection report
Unnamed: 10: Post-completion inspection carried out and recorded, Comparison with schedule of condition documented, Damage (if any) identified and attributed, Remedial works completed and signed off, Final sign-off from adjoining owner, Post-completion report filed

Unnamed: 1: Construction Industry Scheme (CIS) — Registration & Subcontractor Verification
Unnamed: 4: Contractors must register under CIS before making payments to subcontractors. All subcontractors must be verified with HMRC before the first payment. HMRC will confirm the deduction rate: gross (0%), standard (20%), or higher (30% for unregistered). Verification must be refreshed annually or on change of subcontractor details.
Unnamed: 5: HMRC penalties; personal liability for directors; HMRC assessment for unpaid deductions; reputational damage
Unnamed: 6: Before first payment to any subcontractor / On appointment of new subcontractor / Annual refresh
Unnamed: 7: CIS contractor registration confirmation check on project set-up, subcontractor verification reminder on appointment, annual verification refresh alert
Unnamed: 8: Finance team, Procurement team, Subcontractors, HMRC
Unnamed: 9: Confirm CIS contractor registration with HMRC → Verify all subcontractors with HMRC before first payment → Record HMRC verification response and applicable deduction rate → Retain verification records → Refresh verification annually or on change of subcontractor details → Maintain subcontractor CIS status register
Unnamed: 10: CIS contractor registration confirmation, Subcontractor verification records from HMRC for all subcontractors, Deduction rate records per subcontractor, Annual refresh confirmations, Subcontractor CIS status register maintained

Unnamed: 1: Construction Industry Scheme (CIS) — Monthly Returns & Deduction Statements
Unnamed: 4: Monthly CIS returns must be submitted to HMRC by 19th of the month following payment. CIS deductions must be made from labour payments at the verified rate and paid to HMRC monthly. Deduction statements must be issued to all subcontractors monthly. Annual reconciliation at year end.
Unnamed: 5: HMRC penalties: £100 for first month; £200 for 2-12 months; £300 or 5% of deductions (whichever higher) after 12 months; interest on unpaid deductions; potential criminal prosecution for deliberate non-compliance
Unnamed: 6: Monthly — by 19th of following month / Annual reconciliation
Unnamed: 7: Monthly CIS return deadline reminder (19th of month), HMRC payment due date alert, deduction statement issuance reminder
Unnamed: 8: Finance team, Accounts payable, Payroll, HMRC, Subcontractors
Unnamed: 9: Calculate CIS deductions for all subcontractor labour payments each month → Pay deductions to HMRC by 19th of following month → Submit monthly CIS return to HMRC by 19th → Issue monthly deduction statements to all subcontractors → Retain HMRC submission confirmations → Annual CIS reconciliation → Correct any errors promptly
Unnamed: 10: Monthly CIS return submissions with HMRC reference numbers, HMRC payment receipts, Deduction statements issued to all subcontractors monthly, Annual reconciliation completed, No late submissions or payments

Unnamed: 1: Domestic Reverse Charge VAT — Finance Act 2004 / HMRC VAT Notice 735
Unnamed: 4: From March 2021, domestic reverse charge VAT applies to most construction services between VAT-registered businesses in the construction supply chain. Instead of the supplier charging VAT, the customer accounts for it. Suppliers must state on invoices that reverse charge applies. Incorrect treatment of reverse charge is a significant VAT compliance risk.
Unnamed: 5: HMRC penalties for incorrect VAT treatment; VAT assessment; interest on underpaid VAT; reputational damage
Unnamed: 6: All construction services invoices between VAT-registered businesses in the construction supply chain
Unnamed: 7: Reverse charge VAT invoice checker on invoice receipt, supplier VAT registration check on appointment, monthly VAT return review alert
Unnamed: 8: Finance team, Accounts payable, Procurement, HMRC, VAT Advisor, Subcontractors
Unnamed: 9: Confirm VAT registration status of all construction suppliers → Train finance team on domestic reverse charge rules → Implement reverse charge treatment for all qualifying invoices → Ensure suppliers state 'Reverse charge — customer to account for VAT' on invoices → Account for reverse charge VAT on monthly VAT return → Periodic VAT compliance review → VAT advisor review annually
Unnamed: 10: VAT registration confirmations for all qualifying suppliers, Reverse charge treatment applied on all qualifying invoices, VAT return correctly reflects reverse charge, Invoice verification process documented, Annual VAT compliance review completed

Unnamed: 1: Right to Manage — Commonhold & Leasehold Reform Act 2002 — RTM Claim Response
Unnamed: 4: Qualifying leaseholders in eligible buildings have a collective right to acquire management functions without proving fault. Landlord must respond to RTM claim notice within 28 days: serve counter notice if valid grounds exist, or acknowledge the right to manage. If RTM proceeds, management transfers to the RTM company on an agreed date.
Unnamed: 5: First-tier Tribunal determination; loss of management of building; legal costs; reputational damage
Unnamed: 6: RTM claim notice received from qualifying leaseholders
Unnamed: 7: RTM claim notice receipt alert, 28-day response countdown, eligibility assessment reminder
Unnamed: 8: Leasehold Management team, Legal, Finance, Leaseholders, RTM company representatives
Unnamed: 9: Receive and date-stamp RTM claim notice → Assess eligibility: is the building eligible? Do the claimants qualify? → Serve counter notice within 28 days if valid grounds exist → If unable to resist: acknowledge right to manage → Agree management transfer date → Prepare management handover documentation → Transfer all accounts, contracts and documents → Update asset management records
Unnamed: 10: RTM claim notice date-stamped on receipt, Eligibility assessment within 28 days, Counter notice or acknowledgement served within 28 days, Management handover documentation prepared, Asset records updated

Unnamed: 1: Domestic Abuse Act 2021 / Housing Act 2021 (Part 4) — Disclosure & Support Response
Unnamed: 4: Social landlords must: have a domestic abuse policy, train frontline staff, maintain safe referral pathways, and provide appropriate housing solutions for DA victims. Local Authorities must ensure DA victims are not made homeless due to joint tenancy. Powers to end joint tenancies without perpetrator's consent and exclude perpetrators.
Unnamed: 5: RSH regulatory action; Ombudsman maladministration; risk of serious harm to tenants; civil claims; reputational damage
Unnamed: 6: Domestic abuse disclosure by tenant / Joint tenancy with perpetrator identified / Housing allocation for DA victim
Unnamed: 7: DA case creation with safe communication protocol, joint tenancy with perpetrator flag, safe rehousing priority tracker
Unnamed: 8: Housing Officers (DA-trained), Legal, Safeguarding Lead, Local Authority, IDVA/ISVA services, Police, Courts, RSH
Unnamed: 9: Publish domestic abuse policy → Train all frontline staff to DASH risk identification level → Establish safe referral pathway to IDVA services → On disclosure: create case with safe communication protocol → Process joint tenancy exclusion/injunction if required → Prioritise safe rehousing for high-risk victims → Annual policy and training review
Unnamed: 10: Published DA policy, Staff DASH training completion records, Safe referral pathway documented, DA case records with safe communication protocols, Joint tenancy exclusion orders evidenced, Safe rehousing completion records, Annual policy review

Unnamed: 1: Right to Repair Regulations 1994 — Qualifying Repair Response (Secure LA Tenants)
Unnamed: 4: Secure tenants of Local Authorities have a statutory Right to Repair for qualifying repairs under £250. Landlord must complete within prescribed response times (1-7 working days depending on type). If not completed on time, tenant can appoint own contractor. Compensation of £10 minimum rising by £2/day (capped at £50) payable.
Unnamed: 5: Tenant appoints own contractor at landlord cost; compensation of £10-£50; reputational damage; Ombudsman complaint
Unnamed: 6: Qualifying repair request from secure tenant / Response time deadline approaching
Unnamed: 7: Qualifying repair auto-identification, Right to Repair response time SLA alert, compensation trigger on missed deadline

Unnamed: 9: Identify qualifying repair requests (under £250, prescribed categories) → Acknowledge and give expected completion date → Complete within prescribed response time → If unable to complete on time: notify tenant of Right to Repair entitlement → Process tenant's chosen contractor appointment → Pay compensation if applicable → Log all Right to Repair cases
Unnamed: 10: Qualifying repair identification records, Response time compliance log, Right to Repair notifications where applicable, Contractor appointment records, Compensation payment records

Unnamed: 4: On grant of planning permission, extract all S106 obligations, catalogue every obligation, create compliance tracker and assign responsible officers. Every obligation (affordable housing, financial contributions, nominations, viability, monitoring fees) must be individually logged with trigger dates, responsible parties and evidence requirements.

Unnamed: 10: Complete S106 obligations register, Trigger dates and responsible officers assigned, Tracker live and accessible, LPA monitoring officer contact confirmed, Board briefing minutes

Unnamed: 1: Section 106 Agreement — Affordable Housing Tenure Mix Compliance
Unnamed: 4: Delivery must precisely match the S106 tenure mix: units by tenure (social rent, affordable rent, shared ownership, intermediate), split between affordable and market units by phase, and cascade provisions if a specified tenure becomes unviable.
Unnamed: 5: Planning enforcement; injunction; financial penalty; refusal of market unit sales until affordable obligations met; RSH concern

Unnamed: 7: Tenure mix compliance checker at each phase, tenure change request alert for LPA notification

Unnamed: 10: S106 tenure mix schedule confirmed, Unit-by-unit tenure compliance record, RP appointment evidence, LPA tenure compliance confirmation per phase, Deed of variation for any tenure changes, RSH registration of all affordable units

Unnamed: 1: Section 106 Agreement — LA Nomination Rights
Unnamed: 2: Local Planning Authority / Local Authority Housing team
Unnamed: 4: S106 typically grants LA nomination rights for affordable rent units and a proportion of shared ownership. Landlord must notify LA at practical completion, process nominations within agreed timescales, and apply cascade provisions if LA fails to nominate within the agreed period.

Unnamed: 9: Notify LA nominations officer of each unit at practical completion → Provide unit details → Await LA nomination within agreed timescale → Process nomination: eligibility and affordability checks → Apply cascade provision if LA fails to nominate in time → Let or sell per cascade with evidence → Maintain nomination correspondence register → Annual compliance report to Board

Unnamed: 1: Section 106 Agreement — Phased Delivery Milestones
Unnamed: 4: S106 specifies milestone obligations per phase: phased commencement of affordable units, minimum percentages per phase, practical completion of affordable units before or alongside market units, and longstop dates. Market unit sales/occupation restricted until affordable obligations per phase are met.
Unnamed: 5: Enforcement; stop work on market units; injunction preventing market unit sales; refusal of future reserved matters; reputational damage
Unnamed: 6: Each phase commencement / Market unit practical completion / Affordable unit completion / S106 longstop dates
Unnamed: 7: Phase milestone dashboard with RAG status, market unit restriction flag if affordable obligations unmet, longstop date alert (90/30/7 days)

Unnamed: 1: Section 106 Agreement — Viability Review Mechanism
Unnamed: 4: Many S106 agreements include a viability review triggered at a specified stage (e.g. 50% practical completion). An independent viability assessment is required. If viable, additional affordable units or financial contribution may be required. If unviable, obligation may be reduced.
Unnamed: 5: Failure to trigger review may result in LPA enforcement; failure to comply with review outcome may result in financial clawback
Unnamed: 6: Defined construction completion percentage / Specified date in S106 / Material change in development economics
Unnamed: 7: Viability review trigger alert at agreed construction milestone, independent assessor appointment reminder

Unnamed: 1: Section 106 Agreement — RP Appointment & Affordable Unit Transfer
Unnamed: 4: Affordable units must be transferred to an RSH-registered Registered Provider. RP must be identified and appointed at an agreed stage per S106. Transfer must be at agreed price. Developer retains responsibility for ensuring RP appointment and transfer are completed on time.
Unnamed: 5: LPA enforcement; Homes England grant clawback; RSH non-registration; delay in affordable occupation; financial penalties
Unnamed: 6: RP appointment: per S106 milestone / Transfer: at practical completion of each phase

Unnamed: 10: RP confirmed and RSH registration evidenced, Development Agreement executed, RP appointment evidence to LPA, Legal transfer documentation per phase, Transfer confirmation to LPA and Homes England, RSH notification of stock acquisition

Unnamed: 1: Section 106 Agreement — Financial Contributions (Non-AH)
Unnamed: 4: S106 frequently includes financial contributions: highways, public realm, education, healthcare, open space, ecology, monitoring fees. Each has a trigger (typically commencement or first occupation), amount (sometimes index-linked), and payee. Failure to pay by trigger date constitutes S106 breach and can prevent occupation.

Unnamed: 6: Commencement on site / First occupation / Phase commencement / Calendar date trigger

Unnamed: 9: Extract all financial contributions from S106 → Log each with trigger, amount, payee, index-linking basis → Calculate index-linked amounts at trigger → Issue payment instruction in advance → Make payment to correct payee by trigger date → Obtain receipt → Submit payment evidence to LPA → Update S106 compliance register

Unnamed: 1: Section 106 Agreement — S106 Monitoring Fee & LPA Compliance Reporting
Unnamed: 4: S106 agreements typically include an LPA monitoring fee payable at commencement. Periodic compliance reports must be submitted to the LPA. LPA monitoring officer will periodically review compliance and may request evidence.

Unnamed: 6: Commencement on site (monitoring fee) / Annual compliance report due dates / LPA request

Unnamed: 9: Identify monitoring fee and trigger → Pay monitoring fee to LPA on commencement → Maintain S106 compliance register → Prepare periodic compliance reports → Submit to LPA at agreed intervals → Respond to LPA information requests → File all LPA correspondence
Unnamed: 10: Monitoring fee payment receipt, Periodic compliance reports submitted on time, LPA correspondence log, No enforcement action, Positive compliance relationship maintained

Unnamed: 1: Section 106 Agreement — Overage / Clawback Provisions
Unnamed: 4: Where development profit exceeds the threshold established at viability assessment, overage may be payable. Grant clawback applies if grant conditions are not met within the clawback period (typically 5-15 years). At clawback period end, units are released from grant conditions.

Unnamed: 10: Overage provisions logged, RICS calculation submitted, LPA agreement and payment receipt, Grant clawback period end dates recorded, Disposal notifications and recycled grant records

Unnamed: 1: Section 106 Agreement — Board Compliance Reporting & LPA Relationship Management
Unnamed: 4: Ongoing S106 compliance requires periodic Board-level reporting, proactive engagement with the LPA monitoring officer, and prompt notification of any difficulties. The Board must be aware of all S106 obligations and their status. RSH must be notified of any material change to affordable housing delivery.
Unnamed: 5: RSH regulatory concern; LPA enforcement from breakdown in compliance relationship; Board liability for unreported breach
Unnamed: 6: Board reporting cycle / LPA compliance meeting / Material change to programme / Annual S106 review
Unnamed: 7: Quarterly Board compliance reporting reminder, LPA meeting request alert, RSH material change notification flag, annual S106 audit reminder

Unnamed: 9: Present S106 compliance register to Board quarterly → Flag any obligations at risk → Maintain dialogue with LPA monitoring officer → Notify LPA proactively of programme changes → Agree changes via deed of variation → Notify RSH of material changes to affordable housing delivery → Annual full S106 compliance audit → Board sign-off on annual compliance report
Unnamed: 10: Quarterly Board papers with S106 compliance register, LPA monitoring officer meeting records, Proactive LPA notification evidence, Deed of variation records, RSH material change notifications, Annual compliance audit signed off by Board

Unnamed: 1: Equality Act 2010 — Non-Discrimination in Housing Allocation & Service Delivery
Unnamed: 4: Landlords must not directly or indirectly discriminate against anyone with a protected characteristic in housing allocation, tenancy management or service delivery. Annual analysis of lettings data by protected characteristic required to detect indirect discrimination patterns.
Unnamed: 5: EHRC investigation; civil court orders for damages; injunctions; employment tribunal awards; reputational damage; RSH scrutiny
Unnamed: 6: New or revised allocation policy / Lettings criteria change / Service redesign / Complaint from applicant or tenant
Unnamed: 7: Allocation policy EqIA trigger on every policy change, discrimination complaint escalation flag, annual lettings data analysis

Unnamed: 9: Review all allocation policies and lettings criteria for discriminatory provisions → EqIA on all policies before implementation → Annual lettings data analysis by protected characteristic → Train all housing staff on direct and indirect discrimination → Publish non-discriminatory allocation policy → Respond to discrimination complaints within SLA
Unnamed: 10: EqIA completed for all allocation policies, Annual lettings data analysis published, Staff training completion records, Discrimination complaint log with outcomes, No upheld EHRC or Ombudsman findings

Unnamed: 1: Equality Act 2010 — Reasonable Adjustments for Disabled Tenants
Unnamed: 4: Landlords have a duty to make reasonable adjustments to their practices, policies and procedures where a disabled person is placed at a substantial disadvantage. The duty is anticipatory — landlords must not wait for a request. Physical adjustments to common areas, communication format changes, and tenancy management adjustments all apply.
Unnamed: 5: County court claim for damages; injunction; EHRC investigation; reputational damage; compensation awards
Unnamed: 6: Disability disclosure / Reasonable adjustment request / New service introduced / Annual anticipatory duty review
Unnamed: 7: Reasonable adjustment request tracker (receipt to completion), SLA alert for response within 21 days, annual anticipatory review reminder

Unnamed: 9: Publish reasonable adjustment policy → Train all housing officers to recognise and process requests → Log all requests with date received → Assess reasonableness and proportionality → Implement adjustment or document justified refusal → Notify tenant of outcome in accessible format → Annual anticipatory review of all services
Unnamed: 10: Reasonable adjustment policy published, All requests logged with timestamps, Assessment records per request, Implementation evidence or justified refusal documentation, Annual anticipatory review completed

Unnamed: 1: Equality Act 2010 Section 149 — Equality Impact Assessment (EqIA) & PSED
Unnamed: 4: Public authorities must comply with the Public Sector Equality Duty under Section 149. Requires: due regard to eliminate discrimination, advance equality of opportunity, and foster good relations across all nine protected characteristics. EqIAs required for all significant policies, practices and decisions. Must be Board-approved and published.
Unnamed: 5: Judicial review; EHRC compliance notice; RSH Consumer Standards concern; Ombudsman finding; unlawful decision quashed
Unnamed: 6: New policy or procedure / Significant policy change / New development scheme / Budget decision / RSH Consumer Standards review
Unnamed: 7: EqIA requirement trigger on every policy change request, new scheme approval workflow gate, annual PSED compliance review

Unnamed: 9: Screen all new policies and decisions for PSED relevance → Conduct full EqIA where significant impact identified → Consult affected groups → Document findings and mitigations → Board sign-off → Publish EqIA with policy → Monitor actual equality impacts → Annual PSED compliance audit

Unnamed: 1: Equality Act 2010 — Annual PSED Report & Publication
Unnamed: 4: Public authorities must publish equality information annually demonstrating PSED compliance. Must include: equality monitoring data for allocations and lettings, staff equality data, EqIA register, complaints outcomes by protected characteristic, and progress against equality objectives.

Unnamed: 7: Annual PSED publication deadline reminder, equality objectives progress tracker, staff equality data collection reminder
Unnamed: 8: Equalities Officer, HR, Housing Management, Data team, Communications, Board, RSH, Residents
Unnamed: 9: Collect equality monitoring data across all service areas → Analyse by protected characteristic → Review progress against published equality objectives → Draft annual equality information report → Board sign-off → Publish on website in accessible format → Set equality objectives for following year → Notify residents of publication

Unnamed: 1: Equality Act 2010 — Staff Training & Competency
Unnamed: 4: All staff interacting with tenants, applicants or leaseholders must receive training on Equality Act obligations: nine protected characteristics, direct and indirect discrimination, reasonable adjustments duty, harassment and victimisation. Regular refresher training required. Specialist EqIA training for managers.
Unnamed: 5: Discrimination claims where lack of training is aggravating factor; EHRC investigation; Ombudsman maladministration; RSH concern
Unnamed: 6: New staff induction / Annual refresher / Significant policy change / Complaint or incident highlighting training gap
Unnamed: 7: Training completion tracker, overdue training escalation, induction completion gate for new starters

Unnamed: 9: Develop Equality Act training for all staff → Specialist EqIA training for managers → Deliver at induction for all new starters → Annual refresher → Track completion by team → Update content following legislative changes → Record training in HR system

Unnamed: 1: Part M Building Regulations — Category 2 (Accessible & Adaptable Dwellings)

Unnamed: 1: Disabled Facilities Grant — HGCRA 1996 / Regulatory Reform Order 2008 — Consent & Cooperation
Unnamed: 4: Local Authorities have a statutory duty to provide DFGs up to £30,000 (England) for disabled occupants. Social landlords cannot unreasonably withhold consent for adaptations. Landlord consent must be given within reasonable timescales. Cooperation with OT assessment process required.
